Hi @andynoack 
I don't know any software allowing me to record what I expect to with URH, indeed.
That's why the bandwidth parameter is very interesting.

I made this chart with sdr# layout to show the difference beteen the whole rtl-sdr output and the channels i try to listen and record.
![channels](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5690599/25758989/f5ffc446-31d0-11e7-9814-365e0ffa2f4e.png)


Currently, there is no intermediate filtering before recording in URH. The 900 Khz observed band is what is also seen with URH spectrum analyzer.
And then, all different channels are mixed together in the recorder and no message can finally be decoded. As you may see, many channels have the same level and will scramble themselves when no filter is used.

Since rtl-sdr won't accept bandwidth parameter out of the whole band sized by amount of sample per seconds taken, a pass-band filter have to be used to only get the 12.5 KHz bandwidth shown in the bottom left IF spectrum.

I can't use a physical filter plugged between my antenna and rtl-sdr key because the goal of my study is to setup a multi-channel decoder which listen at many of them at the same time.

I wish my knowledge of gnu-r and python signal processing was more developed to help you to make this possible. Let me know if I can provide you anything.

If the underlying gdb process dies you can either refresh the window, or go to the dashboard and click `open tab with new gdb process`. 

I suppose a button could be added to re-spawn a gdb process for the current window, but refreshing accomplishes the same thing, and doesn't require a bunch of cleanup to be done by gdbgui, which might end up being buggy. So I will just recommend refreshing or using the dashboard.

Currently it's not so pleasant to use though, compared to the quick access menu that chrome debugger supplies.

I am very sorry but real-life obligations have prevented my from finding time to work on a release. Unfortunately, I don't see any changes in the next few months. 

Here is a summary of the current release status:

1) @isuruf has provided manylinux and OSX wheels.
2) I have made progress in building Windows wheels locally. There are many warnings that I need to research and verify they are not real bugs.
3) I don't know how to handle the LGPL provision that requires providing the source for any included or statically linked binary files. As I understand the requirements, the easiest (from the compliance perspective) approach is include the source within the binary wheels. But that would increase the file size dramatically. Another option is to provide two source file downloads - one just gmpy2 for distributions and for users compiling against local libraries, and second file with all the various sources. But I don't know how the PyPi / pip ecosystem supports this. (In the old PyPi infrastructure, I could just create another zip file and upload it.)

Summary

Python only has one actually asynchronous exception, and that's
KeyboardInterrupt, which actually can arrive between any bytecodes. Curio's
current design adds two restrictions: cancellations can only be delivered
when a task is yielded (really this is a Python restriction), and the task
being interrupted has to be in a state where it can unwind whatever it was
blocking for without side effects (so e.g. if it was about to take a lock
that's fine but if it just took a lock then we have to wait until the next
yield point). These both seem like good restrictions to me :-). That
article's really interesting, but it makes sense that a functional language
with async cancellation as a runtime primitive ends up with a somewhat
different design than a procedural language without that primitive.

If you're interested in reading about analogous designs, pthread
cancellation is extremely similar to curio's, though it turns out to be
much more annoying to deal with things at the C level. The musl developer's
blog at ewontfix.com has some interesting articles on this.

Let's try to dig at the reasons why someone may prefer one unit over another (it's different for everyone) and how we can express that as simple rule to tell pint how you prefer your number for a given quantity. Hopefully we can find a rule that is consistent across different units (kg, meter, second), units raised to a power (meter), and mixed units (kg*m/s, g*km/s) and that doesn't require user code checking the quantity before informing pint of your preferences.

@JesterEE's suggested `precision`, which I agree that the name seems vague as to what it should do. Users would confuse it for the number of values to display after the decimal, a string formatting operation, not a unit conversion.

The way I would describe how to_compact works is that it performs a unit conversion by choosing the largest unit where the absolute value of the quantity is greater than it equal to 1. For compound units, it follows some order of operations (kg*m/s vs g*km/s) using the kilogram-meter-second system, preferring base units for other units. If quantity is 0, use base units.

In code, I would express this as defining a lower threshold (default=1) used to determine the largest unit. This is essentially the same as @JesterEE'd precision: 10^-prec=thresh, without the undefined fractional precision behavior or bias towards base 10 (binary prefixes, 1000 MiB, 0.977 GiB). (Let's not constrain the API to base 10, since the implementation that uses log10 to quickly pick the largest unit could change in the future.)

    (123*u.micrometer).to_compact(threshold=0.001) == 0.123 millimeter.

The upper threshold is dictated by the minimum of the exponents in the units. For example, meter will have the range [1, 1000) and meter² will have a range [1, 1000000). At 1,000,000 m², it will switch to 1 km². And sometimes the next unit isn't a multiple of 1000. For example, Millimeter-Centimeter-meter-kilometer and microsecond-millisecond-second-minute-hour-day. The current to_compact converts units that are strictly multiples of 1000, but that could change in the future.

I think the reason you prefer 0.123 millimeters over 123 micrometers is because you more regularly use millimeters and you can visualize the value better. On the other hand, someone who regularly works with microscopic things may be able to conceptualize 123 micrometers better than 0.123 millimeters. I prefer 200 ns over 0.200 µs because I routinely work with signals that travel at speeds close to the speed of light, and I can use 1 nanosecond ≈ 1 foot to determine the approximate distance a signal traveled given the elapsed time.

If you prefer 123 km over 0.123 Mm, then specifying the lower threshold isn't enough to get the desired units. If user code has to check the quantity to figure out what the lower threshold should be, it might as well specify the unit as `(1.23e3*u.m).to_compact(unit='km')`.

If a new parameter or context were to be added, it would be mutually exclusive with the existing unit argument of to_compact.

Maybe the maximum unit where the number is greater than 0.001 rule isn't really what we want. Perhaps it would be better to select the unit closest to the base unit: if quantity is less than 1*baseunit, use a lower threshold of 0.001, otherwise  use a lower threshold of 1. This solves the kilometer and megameter problem. But doesn't solve the gram and kilogram problem (then again, 0.98 kg and 980 g are equally understandable to me, so I don't care which to_compact produces as long as it's deterministic). It also doesn't solve the meter² and meter³ problem. The caller of to_compact would need to be aware of the degree of the unit to specify 1e-3 as the lower threshold for units of degree 1, 1e-6 for degree 2, 1e-9 for degree 3 (or maybe 1e-4 to split the difference between a really large number and really small number), etc.

Maybe a better solution to determine the preferred unit is to write a method that wraps to_compact. This is where you could implement the logic that decides that 0.123 mm is preferred over 123 µm, but 123 km is preferred over 0.123 Mm, and when to switch from m² to km², and how to deal with compound units.

Can you propose a function (that everyone can agree on) that can determine what the preferred units are for a given quantity? I don't think this problem can be reduced down to specifying one or two numbers as an argument to a function.

After thinking about it more, I realize that this could be handled quite well with contexts, e.g:

```
@context electricity = elec
    [length] ** 2 * [mass] / [current] ** 2 / [time] ** 3 <-> [length] ** -2 * [mass] **-1 / [current] ** -2 / [time] ** -3: 1 / value
@end
```

I might work something up and send in a PR to have a context like this included by default. In the documentation you use the example of wavelength <-> frequency, which are units that are equivalent only under certain circumstances (hence the use of a special context). Ohms and Siemens are _always_ inversely related, so I guess that's why I expected the conversion to work out of the box.

What I'm saying is that for the general case of reciprocal units, no changes are needed. However, for some specific cases (like ohms <-> Siemens), I might advocate that appropriate conversion rules be added to pint by default. What do you think?

I was investigating similar thing - write to CCCD to enable notifications. But when I enabled DEBUG log level I found that library automatically enables notifications for all properties that support it. So, I'm good with it now. But, hopefully, my little investigation will give you some clues of how to access CCCDs manually (I did not tested it on a pygatt myself).

So, first of all I used gatttool to investigate how to enable notifications there.
(Also inserted my comments between calls, I have doubts about 100% correctness, so if you spot any misleading info, please make me know):

**$ gatttool -I**
**# connect <mac>**
**# primary**
...
attr handle: **0x002e**, end grp handle: **0x0032** uuid: **0000180f**-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb 
...
> 0x180f is a [Battery Service](https://www.bluetooth.com/specifications/gatt/viewer?attributeXmlFile=org.bluetooth.service.battery_service.xml)

**# characteristics 0x002e 0x0032**
handle: 0x002f, char properties: 0x12, char value handle: **0x0030**, uuid: **00002a19**-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb
> 0x2a19 is a [Battery Level Characteristic](https://www.bluetooth.com/specifications/gatt/viewer?attributeXmlFile=org.bluetooth.characteristic.battery_level.xml)

**# char-desc 0x0030 0x0032**
handle: **0x0031**, uuid: **00002902**-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb
handle: 0x0032, uuid: 00002908-0000-1000-8000-00805f9b34fb
> 0x2902 is a [Client Characteristic Configuration Descriptor](https://www.bluetooth.com/specifications/gatt/viewer?attributeXmlFile=org.bluetooth.descriptor.gatt.client_characteristic_configuration.xml
) for service **0x180f**

**# char-write-req 0x0031 0100**
Characteristic value was written successfully

> 0x0100 enables notification, it will be written to handle 0x0031 of CCCD 0x2902 of service 0x180f

..............
Notification handle = **0x0030** value: **5e**

> and after battery level changed you receive notification for "char value handle" 0x0030

So, basically, to do the same with pygatt you could try to get handle number with pygatttool first, then use **char_write_handle** as suggested by @BhargavaRamM . Something like (not tested):
`
adapter.char_write_handle(0x0031, bytearray([0100]))
`

Good that you got it working. Only using UnsafeCommand command can most
likely be improved indeed. Some tips:
- You should be able to replace begin and end Form with an environment
(because that's what it is made for).
- Be sure to use the utils.escape function on all user supplied text when
passing it to UnsafeCommand, otherwise you open yourself up to remote shell
command execution.
- BaseCommand is probably useful as well for you. So you can make a custom
class for some of your often used commands.
- In general try to make classes for all your latex commands that you use
often. That way you can make a more python like api for them. It's fine to
use UnsafeCommand inside some of your custom classes. If you do this it
would be great if you could make a pull request with these classes, so
other people can make pdf forms easily as well.

MY_BASE = 'cn=users,cn=accounts,dc=demo1,dc=freeipa,dc=org'

class Entry(object):
    def __init__(self, connection):
        self.connection = connection
        self.object = ObjectDef(self.object_definition)
        self._reader = None

    @property
    def reader(self):
        if self._reader:
            return self._reader
        else:
            self._reader = Reader(self.connection, self.object, MY_BASE)
            return self._reader

class PersonEntry(Entry):
    object_definition = 'Person'

    def __init__(self, connection):
        super(PersonEntry, self).__init__(connection)
        #schema = ****

c = Connection(MY_SERVER, MY_USER, MY_PASSWORD)

I see how it would be useful, but it is unclear to me at what point the decoding should happen. For example, if decoding happens when parsing the original file, we lose round-trip invariance since what's stored in the db does not match what's in the file. Storing two versions in the db, original and decoded, will add time and complexity and space and I'd rather not go there.

What about decoding when reading from the db? Should the decoding happen when populating the attributes, such that the user never sees the `%2C`? Or decode only when calling `__str__` on the Feature object? Actually, that would break round-trip invariance as well. Or should we do as you show here, decode all individual values for each attribute when accessing the values, but not when calling `__str__`?

Since there's not a clear place to decode that would make sense for all use-cases, I think I would prefer at the moment to be explicit and have it be up to the user to do this when it's needed.

So my suspicion is that the `multiprocess` library may not have been built / installed successfully. Can you please try the following 2 python snippet to see if both of them throw errors?
```python
from multiprocessing import Pool

class C:
    def foo(self, s):
        print(s)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    pool = Pool()
    o = C()
    ar = pool.map_async(o.foo, range(4))
    print(ar.get())
```

```python
from multiprocess import Pool

class C:
    def foo(self, s):
        print(s)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    pool = Pool()
    o = C()
    ar = pool.map_async(o.foo, range(4))
    print(ar.get())
```

The correct behavior would be the first one throws the same error you posted, but the second one should run without problem.

If both snippets give errors on your system, then I suspect `dill` or `multiprocess` has not been correctly installed. You can try if uninstalling and re-installing them will solve the problem (sudo maybe needed for the following commands)
```
pip uninstall multiprocess
pip uninstall dill
pip install multiprocess
```
Observe if there are any errors during the multiprocess installation (It requires a C compiler to compile some C code, if you notice something like "WARNING: The C extensions could not be compiled" in the console, then I think `multiprocess` is not going to be installed correctly).

Hey @Fractally,

To answer your last question, you should always start with the "Run" type of algos. Basically, the Run-type algos will avoid running all the calculation on each day and will only let the stack evaluate when the condition is True (new week in this case). I will add something in the docs pertaining to this effect. 

If you have RunWeekly at the end, then it pretty much isn't doing anything since all the other algos have already run. Think of the algos as a stack. The next Algo in the stack runs if the one before it returns True. RunWeekly returns False (therefore halting the execution of the AlgoStack) unless we are starting a new week. 

As for the changes in quantity, this is basically a rounding error of sorts. The framework currently calculates the commission after determining the amount of shares to buy (since commission depends on number of shares, we have some circularity - for efficiency purposes, the framework currently calculates the quantity first, then the commissions). This can lead to a slightly negative amount of capital in the strategy account, which in turn will lead to a security weight over 100%. Therefore, when we rebalance on day t and we target 100% allocation, we might actually sell a few shares to compensate for this effect. You can examine ```t.weights``` to see what I mean. 

Hope this makes sense,
Phil

#!/usr/bin/env python
# coding: utf-8

import config

from celery import Celery
from datetime import datetime


app = Celery('test')
app.config_from_object(config)

    return u'add task'

@ktbyers As @smnmtzgr wrote this color feature is disabled by default. If you want to use this feature you can enable it per user. So if you want to disable it for scripts you have 3 options
1. You run the script with an different user
2. You don't use the feature
3. Each script checks if there is the color feature enabled. If appplicable the script turns off the feature in the beginning and reenables it when finished.

The last two option feels a little bit dirty to me. The first option may works in many cases. Nevertheless filtering those problematic escape codes seems the clearst solution to me. Basically every command line tool has to filter those escape codes. Since every command line tool strips those escape codes it's not necessarily trivial to determine which Codes are used. Filtering every escape code seems to be much easier.

Scoring is probable the least of the overhead of the entire Subliminal processing.  You're calling Guessit against everything anyway to determine if the video/show matches the query anyway. Why not continue to calculate it then and there anyway? Just don't filter the results unless you want scoring in play.

Honestly, scoring things separately (and then intentionally choosing not to score your results) will shave at most 0.03s off the processing time (estimations of course).
```python
# Keep it simple and built in
if use_scoring:
    for video, subtitles in [ (v,s) for (v,s) in \
           list_subtitles(...) if s.score > user_defined_score ]:
            # this sub is good
```

Huh, that's pretty messed up, and kind of a pain in the ass to work around.

Sadly I don't have a Fidelity login to verify the malformation.  But if they're really breaking the OFX spec, I'd recommend at least trying to submit a bug report to Fido... I'd guess that's their own code, not an off-the-shelf server.

We don't really need much of any information from the OFXHEADER... but if you follow the logic in Parser.OFXTree.parse(), we do need the CHARSET out of the header for OFXv1 in order to decode the OFX payload properly (which fixes some annoying i18n issues for European users of ofxtools).   So you can't just comment out the lines in OFXHeader.parse() after the various readline() calls have advanced the source to the beginning of the OFX payload (which would be my first tactic).

But monkey-patching the OFXHeaderV2 regex is fairly ugly, and will annoy you if you're working with multiple FIs.  Might be your best bet though, ugh.

I'm loathe to introduce too many contortions into library code just to work around one FI who can't follow the simplest basics of a published spec... but I'm open to suggestions.

IMO it's worth the time getting up to speed on git workflow.  It's pretty great, even if your skills are as poor as mine.

If running the kafka brokers inside a container, make sure that it advertises the correct hostnames that are accessible by the clients. If not specified it will use the canonical hostname of the container and that may be an internal one that cannot be used outside of the container.

You can set the advertised hosts with `advertised.listeners` in the server properties, or if you just want to test with an unmodified kafka docker image, you can override the default setting with:
```
bin/kafka-server-start.sh config/server.properties --override advertised.listeners=PLAINTEXT://<accessible-hostname>:9092
```

I believe I mentioned what rom uses each structure for in another of our
threads, and I know I mentioned somewhere out there (maybe not to you

chapter 7 from my book.

Anyway, you can't pass `keygen` and `keygen2` at the same time. That
doesn't work. Never meant to. And at least right now, it is not possible to
order by the column data in the right way to combine both `SIMPLE` and
`IDENTITY` with a single index (I can fix it when I get home in 2 weeks).

The simplest method is to keep the sorting separate from the equality with
an `IndexOnly` column. Also remember, sorting only occurs on a (7 byte)
prefix when using `order_by` on string/text columns thau use
`SIMPLE`/`SIMPLE_CI` in rom.

As for `IndexOnly` and `keygen2`... `IndexOnly` is a fake column meant to
offer secondary+ indexes on columns with an existing index already. But
`keygen2` can be passed to most column types to provide an index on any
data in the model, even duplicating indexes (a waste, but possible). An
example: I will keep multiple `IndexOnly` columns that return the same data
(for a sort) under different conditions. Some conditions (and column names)
might be `just_created`, `needs_processing`, or
`requires_external_cleanup`. The data exists once, different columns give
different contexts obviously, and range queries are easy.

... which leads me to why this doesn't already exist. Long story short
(again, I am not at home, and won't be for another couple weeks, so can't
fix it now), querying over this data is fairly annoying, and I didn't want
to introduce more people to the ugly syntax, if I didn't have to. How ugly?
You have to pass `**{'column_name:<wanted_data>': (<start>, <end>)` to
query a range over an explicit matched string, and similar ugly to
`order_by`.

Pydicom has generally been minimalist in altering data, which would argue for returning YBR and letting the user do conversion if desired. If we convert to RGB, then the photometric interpretation data element would have to be modified to match, and then if the user writes the dataset to file, it will not match the original.  So from these arguments I would generally prefer returning the raw data to the user, but providing conversion functions. Forget this. Naturally just after hitting send I realized what is going on.

The reason for this behavior is that pyodbc uses a prepared statement API that puts the temp table into a lower context:

EXEC sp_prepexec 'SELECT ... INTO #x FROM ...'

As soon as the stored procedure finishes, the temp table's context vanishes along with the temp table. Solution, create the temp table first (un-prepared), then insert into it inside of the prepared statement.

itemID = UUID(_a_uuid_str).int

@fenchu re: 

> I believed all in python3 should be 'utf-8'

No, in Python 3 all strings are **Unicode**. Unicode is the character *set* (integer values named *code points* that represent characters). UTF-8 is an *encoding* (sequences of bytes that represent code Unicode code points). See [this article](http://www.joelonsoftware.com/articles/Unicode.html) for more information.

The Unicode *encoding* that Python 3 itself uses internally is really irrelevant. pyodbc's `setencoding` and `setdecoding` methods are its way of controlling how Unicode *characters* - integer code points - in Python are converted to (and from) the streams of bytes it sends to (and receives from) the ODBC driver. For msodbcsql that *encoding* is UTF16-LE, which is the default encoding that pyodbc uses.

Hi @sameerpallav : 

The last issue you ran into is likely due to the modifications made to the code to support feature_selection with feature_learning. I have a feeling there are one or two assumptions we made in the codebase because of this design decision, that you didn't modify when you manually edited auto_ml. 

That said, I still return back to the original recommendation of binning. If you have a column with 40k categories, the model's never going to learn much from that column. You're essentially throwing away all the signal in that column. At that point, you're left with just a single categorical column, and a single float column. That's not very much data to go off of to predict something, especially because categorical columns are oftentimes not super useful (so the remaining categorical column is likely not super helpful). 

Deep Learning, in particular, relies on using huge amounts of data, both in terms of rows and columns. So only having two columns of data means deep learning is likely not your best bet. A standard gradient boosted algorithm (what auto_ml uses by default) is likely more accurate with this problem. Gradient boosted algorithms tend to be best for most business problems, and are much more easily explainable. 

From here, this sounds not a ton like a modeling problem, and more like a data collection and feature engineering problem. Let me know if you run into any issues with auto_ml, and I'm happy to debug! I appreciate your thorough bug reports.

I agree, the decoding is incorrect.  The `deviceInstanceRangeLowLimit` and `deviceInstanceRangeHighLimit` are required in the sense that in order to decode the `WhoHasLimits` sequence they both need to be provided.  The `limit` element is optional, so when decoding the `WhoHasRequest` it should accept both of them missing and then continue to require the `objectIdentifier` or the `objectName` of the `object`.

There is another case where something similar is happening, the `ReadRangeRequest` has a `range` element which is an optional choice, so if the decoding of the sub-elements fails (like when none of the choices has been specified) the whole thing should still pass.

I think both of these cases can be solved by catching the `InvalidTag` raised during the decode in the section of `constructeddata.py` in `Sequence.decode()` where there is the comment `# some kind of structure` which restores the tag list from its backup and continues on.

> Concrete example : main is colored red (since it's the call tree root), yet it's likely not a good place to look for optimizations.

The way I read these graphs myself is starting from main, then follow the hottest children. That is, I rarely care for the "hot function", but rather the "hot code path". This is because knowing that a function is taking time is rarely enough -- one needs to know the context -- why the control flow goes into there.

For example, imagine a profile where the hottest function is memcpy -- it's probably the most optimized function of the C runtime already -- what one wants to know is which function is abusing of mempcy -- ie, which caller --, and if we color self times it's not obvious, but if we color total times it is.

That said, you're not the first to ask this ( https://code.google.com/p/jrfonseca/issues/detail?id=67 ) , and I can imagine cases where it can indeed be useful, so although I don't have motivation to write it myself, I'm happy to merge such patch.

It's not a trivial change -- you'll need to normalize the ratios by finding the hotest node and dividing all other ratios by it.

Logging output to an x-unit format would help with Jenkins integration without having to write a plugin, it is somewhat of an informal standard for test reporting.  My current employer (and several before it) leveraged this formatting, even for non-java projects.  Sometimes through test runner output format changes, sometimes just transforming the default output format to something x-unit-like, essentially something sufficient enough for Jenkins' reporting plugins to pick up on.

Here is a JUnit Schema for Apache Ant's JUnit, in case you haven't seen it before:

https://github.com/windyroad/JUnit-Schema

I feel like this format, in particular, will help drive broader adoption of this admittedly cool tool.

This issue post over at TravisCI (https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/239) lists several CI systems that expect this sort of format, presented as evidence for my argument.  However, TravisCI doesn't currently support it, from what I can tell with a few minutes of searching.  In fact, there is a bounty open for JUnit style build artifacts that are parsed into a simple report:  https://www.bountysource.com/issues/848108-handle-ant-s-junit-xml-formatted-output-from-test-spec-runs.

None of this is to say a Jenkins plugin wouldn't be completely awesome, but don't forget about all the other CI systems that would get free reporting integration if you were to just add an xunit-style output file (as ci_reporter, NodeUnit, Jasmine, Clojure.test, PyUnit, SimpleTest, JUnit, NUnit have done).  Then you have Hudson/Jenkins, Bamboo, and apparently at some point in the foreseeable future, TravisCI as painless integrators of this framework.

At any rate, to close a long post, we will likely build something internally, either hack at a fork or just transform the output data to a minimally acceptable junit-format for our Jenkins integration, as we have a large and varied body of testing assets spanning languages and testing types that all either already conform to xunit-style outputs or have tooling around them to produce such output from the default output formats.

1) You may be modeling an image with a combination of parametric light profiles and non-parametric pixel grid reconstructions. Regardless of red-shift, you subtract all parametric light profile models off before performing the non-parametric pixel grid reconstruction. Perhaps, therefore the calculation of each D matrix should also be moved to the end of the analysis chain? (D must use the parametric model subtracted images).

2) For strongly lensed quasars, we might have a a parametric and non-parametric model to fit the source galaxy's light. I believe your example above could be solved with an extra return value, which is an index of what an object collided with.

```
a = rays()
b = triangles()
c = a.intersection(b)

for intersection in c:
    geometry, ray, triangle = intersection
```

So the intersection could instead be a tuple of indices with an intersection value.
Which also allows you to do

```
_, ray_indices, triangle_indices = c
intersecting_rays = rays[ray_indices]
triangles = triangles[triangle_indices]
```

Whether the tuple is indices, a masked array of booleans, or what-not isn't important.
But the extra return value should solve your problem?



As an object wrapper, it sounds like there needs to be the ability to
a. the source object
b. the collided object
c. the intersection object (line segment, point, ray, etc)
d. further derivation of intersections, such as ray reflections, sphere distances, etc.

I'm not sure how you'd wrap that as objects.
It seems like you'd want a generic collection that can contain all types, which can perform checks against other collections.
It could then return a collision result, which is a superset of a geometry collection. Which lets you extract ray collisions, etc. It could even return pointers to the original geometry from those collections.
Then if you wanted to do further manipulation such as flipping normals, applying colours, you take the geometry type you want, then run a function over them (flip, colour, etc), then re-insert them in a new collection, that would work?

Curious to see what you're thinking.

Using whitenoise and waitress to serve static files means that the wsgi app serves static files. This means that a worker thread is used for static file download, which isn't great.

Thanks for reporting. There's a story to this.

The first project I developed with the syzygy module is https://syzygy-tables.info. Back then I was using an early version with lazy file opening. Additionally, for such a service, there is no point in ever closing tables.

Initially everything worked fine. But a couple hundred requests later the service stopped working. You can probably guess what happened: The limit for open file descriptors was exhausted, eventually.

I strongly dislike stuff that blows up after a while in production, but not in testing. So this motivated the decision to mmap all the files at startup. This way you can tell there is a problem right away. (More expensive parts of table initialization are still done lazily.)

Check out `ulimit -a | grep descriptors`. You can set a higher limit like `ulimit -n 4096`.

---

I agree that python-chess could handle this better. Maybe a parameter for maximum open file descriptors and closing LRU files would do. This way the user can decide, if it is common for their use case, to open many/all of the tables, or if they only need some.

---

In your code, you wrote `lambda x: ['id']` instead of `lambda x: x['id']`. Since the array is non-empty, it would return true. After fixing that, things seem to work as you would expect.

I took a closer look at the code, and your bug report is correct. Currently, the default `json` writer is used on the `to_json` call which by default uses utf 8. The `jsonl` writer uses the builtin `open` and its default encoding which is the system default.

My proposal to fix this issue would be:

Ansible-cmdb generally uses Ansible's `hosts`file and the output of Ansible's fact gathering. The facts are stored in the `out` directory when you run the following command:

    mkdir out
    ansible -m setup --tree out/ all

I suspect that the `hosts` file or in one of the files in the `out` directory contains some unicode characters (like é or ç or something) that Ansible-cmdb doesn't like. Figuring out where exactly the problem is is kinda painful. You can try calling ansible-cmdb without the `-i hosts` argument or by temporary moving the `hosts` file to another location. If it still doesn't generate, then the problem is in the `out/` dir. I usually just delete files from the `out` dir until it works to narrow down which host is causing the problems.

If you want to, you can send a zip file with the `hosts` and `out/` files to my email address (ferry.boender@gmail.com), and I could have a look for you. The data you send me would be treated highly confidential and will be securely wiped once the problem is fixed.

**2. Will it replace flat files or can we have both flat files and mongodb in such a way if someone dont have db installed can still use flat files conversations ?**

In the branch i've been working on, I replaced the flat file access completely because I didn't have a way to maintain the ability to store object references for each statement. Future changes that need to be made to this program really require a database of some kind, and csv files don't provide an adequate level of flexibility. For instance, the database implementation will allow the bot to query responses that are specific to a single user, or recall conversations that it had with a particular person.

For this, I've added a method called `train()` which takes a list of usernames and statements as a parameter. I've included a sample below. In the future, it should also be possible to pass a file in as a parameter to the training method.

```
from chatterbot import Terminal
from chatterbot.algorithms.engram import engram

# Create a new terminal chat bot and use the engram algorithm to select responses.
chatbot = Terminal(engram)

conversation = [
    ["user", "Hello"],
    ["salvius", "Hi there!"],
    ["user", "How are you doing?"],
    ["salvius", "I'm great."],
    ["user", "That is good to hear"],
    ["salvius", "Thank you."],
    ["user", "Your welcome."],
    ["salvius", "Sure, any time."],
    ["user", "Yeah"],
    ["salvius", "Can I help you with anything?"]
]

# Train the chat bot on these know conversations
chatbot.train(conversation)

# Start the terminal session
chatbot.begin()
```

That's the normal behavior for a lot of skills, if I'm understanding your question. 

For example, if you say "open MySkill" or "start MySkill" (and you don't have a session open or any kind of data persistence going on), you'll hit the LaunchRequest intent. Mine gives some helpful hints about what you can ask for/say. (As the voice design guidelines suggest.)

If you say "ask MySkill to do X" then it will route directly the intent for X based on your Utterances. I.e. you don't have to first say "start MySkill". The act of matching an Utterance and sending an Intent implicitly triggers the skill and gives it the right information for Flask-Ask to route it to your specific intent. 

If you're saying you don't want to say "ask MySkill" at the beginning, then you have to either have an open session (like being in the middle of your example game skill), or be one of the special integrations (like for smart home stuff) that can handle bare commands like "Alexa turn off living room lights".

Hi @znorris,

Are you using the insert key of the keyboard? If so, that's interesting, because I think, I never tested it. My keyboard does not have insert keys, and I use 'i' or 'a' to go to insert mode, and 'escape' to go back.
I guess we have to fix that.

The ':q' issue for the help should also be fixed. That's easy.

Anyway, I'd say it's still experimental to some extend. All features that are developed are supposed to be stable and bug free. Bugs in existing features have to be fixed. Many features are however still missing, but I'm using it quite often as my own editor without any issues.

There is however one big issue, that is the handling of big files. (>2000 lines of code). Editing such big files becomes slow. I know why. I know how to solve it. But not how to do it in pure Python code, and not without loosing compatibility with Pygments. And not without risking to introduce many small bugs.

Data structures are really important for editors when files are bigger. The underlying library ``prompt-toolkit`` was meant as a readline replacement, and not designed for such big files. The data structures of ``prompt-toolkit`` are extremely simple. This was good enough for most use cases, and allows for very quick development. So, I'm honest, currently ``prompt-toolkit`` is not powerful enough to compete with a real Vim or Emacs. Maybe some day it will, but it's not a priority. But for now, it should certainly work fine for smaller stuff and simple editing.

```
Text('[[Image:HousingAuthority-HousingAuthority.jpg|thumb|right|300px|An example of a configuration created using HousingAuthority]]

'''HousingAuthority''' is a library used to make easy configurations that go into OptionHouse.

==Table data==
===frameData===
;Fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| frame || frame || frame || Frame you want all of the HA widgets to go onto, if no frame is passed then a blank unnamed one is created.
|-
| columns || number || 1 || Display style, 1 is a one by one style, 2 will be 2 widgets per a row and so on
|-
| handler || table || nil || Handler for all callbacks to use, all callbacks require set/onSet/get to be passed as a string if this is set.
|-
| set || function/string || nil || The set callback that all widgets inherit
|-
| onSet || function/string || nil || The onSet callback that all widgets inherit
|-
| get || function/string || nil || The get callback that all widgets inherit
|}
;Remarks
You only need to pass the frame if you're doing some sort of custom modification to it, otherwise you can just skip it and it'll handle everything.

If you do pass a frame, do not rely on it being the one you should register with OptionHouse if your widgets are going to overlap off the OptionHouse display frame we wrap it a scroll frame around it.

===widgetData===
All HousingAuthority widgets (input, color, dropdown, slider, checkbox, button) accept these by default as fields so aren't listed.

;Fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| text || string || <required> || Text shown next to the widget
|-
| group || string || nil || Used for grouping widgets together
|-
| var || string/number/table || <required> || Used for identifying widgets, passed for all set/onSet/get/validate functions
|-
| help || string || nil || Shows a little "[?]" next to each widget in green, handy if you need to clarify or give extra information that can't be explained in the text alone.
|-
| handler || table || nil || Handler function for using in the callbacks require set/onSet/get to be passed as a string if this is set.
|-
| set || function/string || <required> || Function called when a widgets configuration value changes
|-
| onSet || function/string || nil || Same as set, however you should only use this if you need to know when something changes not changing the configuration
|-
| get || function/string || <required> || Called when we're loading the widget and when the frame is shown, the actual value of the widget
|-
| order || integer || 999999 || Order to display the widgets in
|}

;Remarks
'''Grouping'''

Grouping is a way to break up configuration so it doesn't look like a single mass of options, if one widget has the group field passed all of them must, the value should be the text that you want displayed, for example the below will show an input and color widget inside a frame labeled "Frame" and a check inside a frame labeled "General".

 { { type = "input",
     group = "Frame",
     <input data>
   }
   { type = "color",
     group = "Frame",
     <input data>
   }
   { type = "check",
     group = "General",
     <input data>
   } }

'''Callbacks'''
These are how the callback functions are handled along with arguments used.

''No handlers passed''

set(var, value)
onSet(var, value)
validate(var, value)
get(var)

''Handler passed''

handler[set](handler, var, value)
handler[onSet](handler, var, value)
handler[validate](handler, var, value)
handler[get](handler, var)

==HousingAuthority API==
===HA:RegisterFrame([frameData])===
;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type !! Details
|-
| HAObj || table || HousingAuthority data object for registering widgets
|}

===HA:CreateConfiguration(widgets[, frameData])===
;Args
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type !! Details
|-
| widgets || table || List of all the widgets you want to add
|-
| frameData || table || Frame data 
|}
;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type !! Details
|-
| frame || frame || Frame that should be registered with OptionHouse, this is just the results of [[#HAObj:GetFrame.28.29|HAObj:GetFrame()]]
|}

;Types
List of widget types that can be used

{| class="darktable"
|-
! Type !! Called Function
|-
| group || [[#HAObj:CreateGroup.28data.29|HAObj:CreateGroup()]]
|-
| input || [[#HAObj:CreateInput.28data.29|HAObj:CreateInput()]]
|-
| dropdown || [[#HAObj:CreateDropdown.28data.29|HAObj:CreateDropdown()]]
|-
| color || [[#HAObj:CreateColorPicker.28data.29|HAObj:CreateColorPicker()]]
|-
| check || [[#HAObj:CreateCheckBox.28data.29|HAObj:CreateCheckBox()]]
|-
| button || [[#HAObj:CreateButton.28data.29|HAObj:CreateButton()]]
|-
| slider || [[#HAObj:CreateSlider.28data.29|HAObj:CreateSlider()]]
|-
| label || [[#HAObj:CreateLabel.28data.29|HAObj:CreateLabel()]]
|}

;Remarks
Most of the time, all you want is to call this and just pass the table with all the configuratiions, if you need more flexability you can use the below HAObject API's.

Tables should look like this,

 { { type = "input",
     <input data arguments>
   },
   { type = "dropdown",
     <dropdown data arguments>
   },
 }

and so on.

==HousingAuthority Object API==
===HAObj:GetFrame()===
;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type !! Details
|-
| frame || Frame/ScrollFrame || Frame with all the widgets displayed in it, that should be registered with OptionHouse
|}

;Remarks
Once you call this, you can no longer call any of the widget creation functions, if the total height of all the widgets is creater then 280 then we wrap the passed frame around a ScrollFrame and return the ScrollFrame instead.

===HAObj:GetObject(frame)===
Returns the HAObj that created the frame that was passed in.

;Args
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Name !! Type !! Details
|-
| Frame || frame || HA display frame thats given when calling [[#HAObj:GetFrame.28.29|HAObj:GetFrame()]]
|}

;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type !! Details
|-
| HAObj || table || Useful if you want to update a dropdown thats already beeen created, but you created it using [[#HA:CreateConfiguration.28widgets.5B.2C_frameData.5D.29|HAObj:CreateConfiguratiion()]
|}

===HAObj:CreateGroup(data)===
;Data fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| background || table || rgb of 0.094117 || Backdrop color of the group, by default this is a blackish gray
|-
| border || table || rgb of 0.4 | Backdrop border color of the group, by default this is gray
|}

;Remarks
This is a way to configure how your group frames will look, not required if you're using grouping due to the defaults, you should pass the tables formatted as { r = #, g = #, b = # } for both background and border.

===HAObj:CreateDropdown(data)===
Dropdown for selecting a list of options, useful when you don't want them to be able to enter anytihng like in an input 

;Data fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| text || string || <required> || Actual text to display
|-
| default || string/number || nil || Default value to select by key, if the default doesn't match the list we use the first row
|-
| list || table || <required> || List of rows to shown, must be formatted as { { "key1"", "text1" }, { "key2", "text2" } } and so on
|}

;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type
|-
| dropdownFrame || Frame
|}

===HAObj:UpdateDropdown(data)===
Allows you to update an already created dropdown with a new default and list

;Data fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| var || table/string/number || <required> || Used for identifying the droopdown to be updated
|-
| default || string/number || nil || Default value to select by key, if the default doesn't match the list we use the first row
|-
| list || table || <required> || List of rows to shown, must be formatted as { { "key1"", "text1" }, { "key2", "text2" } } and so on
|}

;Remarks
You should only call this when you know the dropdown is hidden, meanning either OnShow for the frame or in response to set/onSet, if you try and call it with it open then it'll show the old rows followed by the new ones.

var is used for identify the dropdown, if the dropdown is a table then the values of each must match 100%, if you have multiple dropdowns with the same var then it'll update the first it finds.

===HAObj:CreateLabel(data)===
Adds a simple text that can be used to break up configuration to prevent them all looking clustered together, can be used in place or grouping or with it if need be.

;Data fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| text || string || <required> || Actual text to display
|-
| color || table || nil || Text color, should be passed as { r = #, g = #, b = # }
|-
| font || fontInstance || GameFontNormal || Font instance to inherit from
|-
| fontPath || string || nil || Font file to use when displaying the text, by default will grab from GameFontNormal
|-
| fontSize || number || nil || Size of the font, can only be used if you pass fontPath too
|-
| fontFlag || string || nil || Display flags, see FontInstance:SetFont() for possible arguments, requires fontPath and fontSize to be used
|-
| xPos || number || 8 || Allows finer control over how far other widgets are from the label
|-
| yPos || number || 5 || Allows finger control on how far this widget is from either the side or another widget
|}

;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type
|-
| label || FontString
|}

===HAObj:CreateColorPicker(data)===
Creates a small button that can be used for setting colors, the middle of the button is the currnet color used

;Data fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| default || table || nil || Default color to use if get() returns nil
|}

;Returns
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Return !! Type
|-
| button || Button
|}

;Remarks

Simple check box

===HAObj:CreateInput(data)===
Text or number inputt field

;Data fields
{| class="darktable"
|-
! Field !! Type !! Default !! Details
|-
| width || number || 120 || Width of the input box
|-
| default || string/number || nil || Default value inside the input
|-
| realTime || boolean || false || By default, we only consider the input box changed when focus is lost or enter is pressed, you can change it using this to be considered changed whenever the text changes or enter is pressed
|-
| maxChars || number || nil || Maximum number of characters that can be entered
|-
| numberic || boolean || false || Only allows entering of numerical characters into the input box
|-
| validate || string/function || nil || Allows you to restrict whats considered valid iinput
|-
| error || string || nil || Shows a red "[!]" next to the widget when validate returns nil, string format is called on this, so you can also show the value entered in the error message
|}

;Remarks
Validate allows you to restrict thats considered acceptable, if it returns nil then it's considered rejected  and a red "[!]" will appear using the message you provided from the error field, once validate returns anything except nil then set/onSet is called.

 local function validateInput(var, value)

 end

What you see is the intended behaviour. The HTML tags are imbalanced in your code sample, so mwparserfromhell treats the offending segment as just text.

A real library like lxml would simply fix the imbalanced tags for you, for comparison. But mwparser doesn't because we require parsing not to transform the text, i.e. you can take the output of `mwparserfromhell.parse(...)` and copy-paste it back into the edit window and MediaWiki will not record an extra revision.

As a reference point, here's a quick informal benchmark of `django-admin cities --force --import=country,region` on my workstation:

```
SQLite (master):           real 0m55.530s, user 0m5.380s, sys 0m1.584s
SQLite (this branch):      real  0m2.965s, user 0m2.812s, sys 0m0.124s

PostgreSQL (master):       real 0m16.668s, user 0m4.000s, sys 0m0.240s
PostgreSQL (this branch):  real  0m4.638s, user 0m2.956s, sys 0m0.116s
```

This means an overall reduction to 5% (SQLite), and 27% (PostegreSQL) of the time taken before. (The benchmark is on a SSD, so the difference with a non-SSD is likely to be greater.)

For bigger imports, without the transaction wrapper, the CPU time stays very noticeably dominated by disk I/O; with the transaction, the bottleneck is reduced to primarily the Python code execution.

Reason why it checks agains a model is because you are using a `ModelFilterSet` which gets the fields from the model definition in order to get the form class for the filter to use for data validation. Meanwhile you should be able to manually define a filterset for that field. Something like:

```python
class MyFilterSet(ModelFilterSet):
    article_count = Filter(form_class=forms.IntegerField())
    class Meta(object):
        model = Reporter
```

you can always see what filters are set on the filterset by doing a representation of the filterset:

```python
print(repr(MyFilterSet()))
```

> The algorithm is based on a balanced binary tree, which allows the computation to be done in O(n log n) time.

which is probably where I got the idea.

Today I found out that there's actually documentation for that code! It apparently uses a clever simplification of my suggestion for a balanced binary tree. Instead of moving nodes around in the tree, you can just keep track of whether each node in the tree is "occupied yet", and use that info when you're updating the array of subtree sizes. That should make it pretty simple to implement except for possibly handling ties.

Let's image this, Some one uses `datemath` to create a REST API which supports querying metrics through query argument like what ElasticSearch, eg. `from=now/w&until=now/w`. So the programer will get these two arguments then translates them into a `datetime` object or `Arrow` object to get datas from database.

The most easy way I can think of is something like:

```
>>> from = 'now/w'
>>> start = dm(from)  # 2017-03-13 00:00:00
>>> until = 'now/w'
>>> end = dm(until, ceil=True)  # 2017-03-19 23:59:59
# select ... from ... where time >= :start and time <= :start
```

every think looks good. But if programer need to modify the argument to implement `ceil()`, the codes may be something like:

```
>>> from = 'now/w'
>>> start = dm(from)
>>> until = 'now/w'
>>> one_seond_before_next_week = until.split('/')[0] + '+1w/w-1s'
>>> end = dm(one_second_before_next_week)
.....
```

The way I managed this is by using filter_backends in the views.

```
from .filter import MyFilter

class MyViewSet(GenericViewSet):
  filter_backends = (MyFilter,)
    
    ...

in filter.py

class MyFilter(BaseFilterBackend):
  def get_schema_fields(self, view):
    fields = [
      coreapi.Field(name="access_key", description="Access Key", required=True, location='query'),
      ...
    ]

    return fields
```
The nice thing is that these filters are composable so you can add more filters to `filter_backends = ` and they will all show up as query parameters.

Not sure if this is the best answer but it's working at least.

There were long discussions with mmerickel over IRC while working on this, where we specifically discussed this use of `reify`, so I trust I did not abuse its purpose.

This is from Pyramid's documentation for `reify`:

> It operates almost exactly like the Python `@property` decorator, but it puts the result of the method it decorates into the instance dict after the first call, effectively replacing the function it decorates with an instance variable. It is, in Python parlance, a non-data descriptor.

So when we `del` the key from the instance dict, we get access to the non-data descriptor back. Not magic, not reliant on how `reify` works internally, just how non-data descriptors work in Python. Here it's caching the value until invalidate, deleting it on invalidate, and caching it again on next access. I don't see your idea of the "whole purpose" of `reify`, that it must be a one-time thing only, mentioned anywhere in the docs. Looks fine to me.

Benchmark script:
```Python
import mxnet as mx
import sys
import os
import scipy
import numpy as np
from mxnet.test_utils import rand_ndarray, assert_almost_equal
import time

def measure_cost(repeat, a, b, out=None):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(mx.nd.elemwise_add(a, b, out=out))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def measure_fallback(repeat, a):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(a.tostype('default'))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def main():
    shape = (1000000, 512)
    context = mx.gpu(0)
    # context = mx.cpu()
    for lhs_density in [0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001, 0.000]:
        mx_lhs = rand_ndarray(shape, stype='row_sparse', density=lhs_density).as_in_context(context)
        mx_lhs_dns = mx_lhs.tostype('default')
        for rhs_density in [0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001, 0.000]:
            mx_rhs = rand_ndarray(shape=shape, stype='row_sparse', density=rhs_density).as_in_context(context)
            mx_rhs_dns = mx_rhs.tostype('default')
            #warmup
            sparse_cost = 0.0
            dns_cost = 0.0
            np_lhs = mx_lhs_dns.asnumpy()
            check = mx.nd.elemwise_add(mx_lhs, mx_rhs)
            np_lhs = np_lhs + mx_rhs.asnumpy()
            assert_almost_equal(check.asnumpy(), np_lhs, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
            mx.nd.waitall()
            for i in range(100):
                sparse_cost += measure_cost(1, mx_lhs, mx_rhs)
                dns_cost += measure_cost(1, mx_lhs_dns, mx_rhs_dns)
            print("%.2f %% %.2f %%" % (lhs_density*100, rhs_density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)

    for rhs_density in [1.000, 0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001, 0.000]:
        mx_lhs_dns = mx.nd.ones(shape, ctx=context)
        mx_lhs = mx_lhs_dns.tostype('row_sparse')
        mx_rhs = rand_ndarray(shape=shape, stype='row_sparse', density=rhs_density).as_in_context(context)
        mx_rhs_dns = mx_rhs.tostype('default')
        #warmup
        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        np_lhs = mx_lhs_dns.asnumpy()
        mx.nd.elemwise_add(mx_lhs, mx_rhs, out=mx_lhs)
        np_lhs = np_lhs + mx_rhs.asnumpy()
        assert_almost_equal(mx_lhs.asnumpy(), np_lhs, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(100):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(1, mx_lhs, mx_rhs, out=mx_lhs)
            dns_cost += measure_cost(1, mx_lhs_dns, mx_rhs_dns, out=mx_lhs_dns)
        print("%.2f %% %.2f %%" % (1.00000*100, rhs_density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)

    for lhs_density in [1.000, 0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001, 0.000]:
        mx_rhs_dns = mx.nd.ones(shape, ctx=context)
        mx_rhs = mx_rhs_dns.tostype('row_sparse')
        mx_lhs = rand_ndarray(shape=shape, stype='row_sparse', density=lhs_density).as_in_context(context)
        mx_lhs_dns = mx_lhs.tostype('default')
        #warmup
        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        np_rhs = mx_rhs_dns.asnumpy()
        mx.nd.elemwise_add(mx_lhs, mx_rhs, out=mx_rhs)
        np_rhs = np_rhs + mx_lhs.asnumpy()
        assert_almost_equal(mx_rhs.asnumpy(), np_rhs, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(100):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(1, mx_lhs, mx_rhs, out=mx_rhs)
            dns_cost += measure_cost(1, mx_lhs_dns, mx_rhs_dns, out=mx_rhs_dns)
        print("%.2f %% %.2f %%" % (1.00000*100, lhs_density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

@v-sukt your commit doesn't. Please reread the guidelines and look at other PRs (that have been closed) as an example.

The current changes break the `HdfsSensorRegex` and `HdfsSensorFolder` subclasses in `airflow.contrib.sensors`, which essentially add some extra filtering behaviour to the base `HdfsSensor`.

One thing that wasn't clear to me about the `HdfsSensor` itself, is whether the sensor checks for an exact file path (or directory), or some pattern. If it should check for the exact path, then the used approach of using ls and filtering doesn't make sense to me, as there can only be a single result. If it checks for a pattern (using glob for example), then I don't understand the need for the regex sensor, as most patterns should be checkable via globs. Similarly, the sensor folder could be replaced by a glob checking for files in directory, with an optional complement argument if we need an empty directory. 

Besides this, the subclasses duplicate most of the code used in the base class for checking file existence. A good way to avoid this would be to allow users to supply an arbitrary function (or list of functions) for filtering down potential file paths. This may however break the current interface.

@pbauer This test isolation issue is most likely not caused by that single test but it is rather a sign that the test fixture or that package itself is causing trouble. Therefore skipping the test is not the best solution, since we just choose to ignore that problem until the next person runs into it. That person then needs to discover the problems that are hidden because people skipped or removed tests before. There is no easy way to fix test isolation problems and skipping tests does not solve any test isolation issue, it just makes things worse.

What I usually do to try those kind of things is I try to find two packages that have an isolation issue and run them together with:

bin/test -s plone.app.contenttypes -s plone.app.linkintegrity

When I can't see an obvious problem with the fixtures (like using functional instead of integration fixture, etc.), I start to play around with the test code to see if I can find the cause for the problem.

The circular ref would be caused by the bound method holding a reference to the instance, and instance holding a reference to the bound method. It's not the end of the world, and the garbage collector would probably get to it eventually (unless the instance has a `__del__` method). That said, circular refs should be avoided when possible because they delay the cleanup of objects (forcing it upon the GC) and are generally a sign of a code-smell.

Given the defined priority order:

> 1. program defaults (`trait.default_value`, *dynamic_defaults*),
> 2. config-files,
> 3. env-var,
> 4. command-line arguments,
> 5. values directly assigned.

This PR cannot achieve the correct ordering of `env-var` without introducing much more complicated changes than I think we should allow. The priority resolves correctly for the Application object itself, but not any of the objects instantiated later by the Application. It can only reliably achieve env higher than all config or lower than all config, due to the use of a single config object.

Consider the order of events for initializing an application with configurables:

1. load CLI arguments into `app.config`
2. load config files into `app.config`, allowing CLI config to take priority
3. instantiate configurable objects with `parent=app`, loading the app config

At this point, all objects receive a single config object with values that should be both higher and lower priority than env, with no way to distinguish them. These instantiations are deeply nested down the chain of all objects, and happen at arbitrary points in time. The only guarantee is that there is a single config object being passed around. 

So the only achievable goal I see is to pick between:

- env variables override all config (breaks several APIs)
- all config overrides env variables

The only thing that seems achievable and consistent is traitlets-level env-defaults that `traitlets.config` is not aware of. This behavior is consistent and understandable, and already in wide use today, if slightly tedious. Addressing that inconvenience is all that we are after with #99. Solving the priority issue would be nice, but doesn't seem doable without introducing significant new problems.

> Still, i haven't understand what is the penalty for hooking in the new functionality in the config layer?

It's not that there's a penalty, it's that implementing it via two systems makes it more complicated and more difficult to implement correctly, as illustrated by the fact that this PR doesn't implement the priority it aims to, and I can't see a way for it to accomplish that without much more complicated changes.

Here is [a test](https://gist.github.com/minrk/bb359a56039eabde31c388250cbfe9e4) demonstrating several very basic cases where the priority resolution of this PR is not as intended.

In every case, the foo and app sources should match because they have config defined at the same levels.

> Let me be clear on this: existing clients don't see ANY side-effects!

I think this is a lot harder to say than that. Jupyter applications instantiate many objects that can be provided by extensions. If those extensions, which are already using the existing `@default` implementation of environment variable defaults (the goal of #99), it is important that they behave in a consistent way, with no changes to the Application object that instantiates them.

I am using django 1.7 with perfectly valid managers, and the monkey patch adds the warning "`Manager.get_query_set` method should be renamed `get_queryset`".
After some inspection, it appears that at some point during the django init, the Manager class gets to a point where ``Manager.__dict__.get("get_query_set")`` is ``<unbound method Manager.get_queryset>`` and ``Manager.__dict__.get("get_queryset")`` is ``None``.

Disabling the monkey patch removes the warning, but doing the following also does !! :
```python

try:
    Manager.get_query_set = Manager.get_queryset
    Manager.get_queryset = Manager.get_queryset  # yep !
except AttributeError:
    Manager.get_queryset = Manager.get_query_set
```

For the time being, XML documents are considered as plain text, not markup. I don't think it can be done as Regexp tokenizer in Preprocess Text widget just does simple selection (without sufficient lookback, backreferences, or replacement).

If you're on Unix-like system, you can filter the XML through a pipeline such as:
```bash
cat myfile.xml | grep -oP '<(\w+)>.*?</(\1)>' | sed -r 's,<(\w+)>(.*)</\1>,\2,g'
```
For your above example, this will print:
```text
Nothing important
Acta Dermato-Venereologica
```
If you have multiple such files in a directory:
```bash
mkdir -p extracted
for file in *.xml; do cat "$file" | ... > "extracted/$file.txt"; done

@s-alexey I discovered these problems: 

- [ ] I noticed a huge slowdown comparing to current version of preprocessing. Take `friends-transcripts` data set and use Word & Punctuation, Porter Stemmer and English stopwords. This configuration on current version takes about 40s but on this PR it requires about 5 minutes and a half. That's about 8 times slower. Can you investigate a bit? If the slowdown is the result of precise progress bar than we should find some compromise.

- [ ] Total tokens in info box does not change if I use filtering by Document frequency or Keep most frequent tokens. It should correspond the final filtered version IMO. 

- [ ] I would also add the `Unique tokens: XXX` information, which is actually the vocabulary size. It is useful to know how many features will BoW create and I think these two statistics (number of tokens and vocabulary size) are usually used to reported the size of a corpus.

- [ ] Using stowords in filtering seems to break `Document frequency` and `Keep most frequent tokens` filtering. For example take `bookexcerpts` pass it through preprocessing and then into Word Cloud (which comes in handy since it prints the number of unique words). If I do not use English stopwords and set to Keep to 50 this works OK and WordCloud also shows 50 words. If I also enable English Stopwords and keep Keep=50 I see 11592 words in WordCloud but it should show 50. Seems like by enabling stopwords  and running preprocessing once Document and Keep filtering are broken forever, i.e. they do not work any more even if I disable stopwords.

- [ ] Rename `Keep most frequent tokens:` to something like `Keep top tokens by document frequency:`

- [ ] Could we a tooltip to Document frequency explaining that it accept whole numbers and fractions.

The current rule works like this (if pyenv is not present):

1. `--three` is essentially a shorthand for `--python=3` (and `--two` is `--python=2`).
2. If `--python=X` is specified, look for `python{X}` in PATH (if `X` is `^\d+(\.\d+)?`; otherwise it is treated as a path).
3. Otherwise, if `requires.python_version = Y` look for `python{Y}` in PATH.
4. Otherwise use whatever Python Pipenv is on (`sys.executable`).

The logic is relatively straightforward by itself IMO, but not really what users would expect.

That behavior is as expected. Here's the help for `iter_entry_points`:

```
Help on method iter_entry_points in module pkg_resources:

iter_entry_points(group, name=None) method of pkg_resources.WorkingSet instance
    Yield entry point objects from `group` matching `name`

    If `name` is None, yields all entry points in `group` from all
    distributions in the working set, otherwise only ones matching
    both `group` and `name` are yielded (in distribution order).
```

It says "only ones matching". It doesn't describe what `matching` means precisely, but looking at the code, it means "matches exactly". So when you specify "bpython" as the name to retrieve, it retrieves just that one.

If you had instead specified `iter_entry_points('console_scripts', 'bpython-curses')`, you'd get a different result:

```
~ $ rwt bpython -q -- -c "import pkg_resources; print(list(pkg_resources.iter_entry_points('console_scripts', 'bpython-curses')))"
[EntryPoint.parse('bpython-curses = bpython.cli:main')]
```

I just started using pyKafka and ran into the topic encoding "surprise". Some thoughts:

For topic and group names it shouldn't be a problem: all acceptable characters are in the ASCII range so converting between `str` and `bytes` is easy.

For sending messages it's also not that hard: we could default to using UTF-8 encoding to convert to `bytes` when a `str` is sent (on Py3, on Py2 we can do the same for `unicode`). That would probably work for most users, and users that want control can send `bytes`. Sending `str` is then just a convenience.

The receiving side is harder: we could try to decode UTF-8, but then some messages might accidentally be valid UTF-8 and the user won't be able to rely on what they are getting. Wanting to receive `bytes` and then suddenly getting a `str` because the bytes happen to be valid UTF-8...

And I think we should keep the API symmetrical. If we can't automatically determine the right thing to do on the receiving side we shouldn't do 'magic' stuff on the sending side. Otherwise users will be surprised when they send strings but receive bytes.

And then we end up at jofusa's suggestion :-)

Well for me, having the cake and eating it would amount to:

- if there is a zero return code, custodian acts like it did before (this should already be the case)
- if there is a nonzero return code, custodian again acts like it did before. Practically, this would be simple and straightforward to implement, and amount to checking the return code only inside the validation loop of custodian.py (~line 414) and making the return code a validator. Hi,

I am new to scikit-image and I want to contribute. I have worked in image processing in matlab and openCV.  I was familiarising myself with the scikit-image codes and I sort of followed up this issue about swirl.
This issue had come up in 2013. 

The explanation given at that time was that the swirl requires information beyond the borders of the current image. So, skimage.transform.swirl pads before transforming. The padding is done with zeros which causes what we see in the demo.

It was also stated that changing modes rescue the behaviour. The change suggested in the code was in this particular line:

im = skimage.transform.swirl(im, mode='reflect')

I tried to run my code changing the line. And, I could see the difference. I hope this was useful.
I would like to contribute and will be grateful if somebody could give me a few starting pointers.

> Do you have benchmarks with openCV vs Pillow-SIMD?

https://python-pillow.org/pillow-perf/

> Is jpeg compression SIMD optimized?

This depends on your JPEG codec installed in the system and compiled with Pillow. Many Linux distros use libjpeg-turbo by default which is highly optimized.

> Are all the resizing methods SIMD optimized?

No, Nearest neighbor method and [affine transformations](https://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/5.0.0/reference/Image.html#PIL.Image.Image.transform) are not SIMD-optimized.

Well that's because the actual determination of the number of "pool connections" that are initialized as "connected" (actually _close to_ connected), is determined by a function and isn't a hard fast value ([see here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1137)).

Reading over the comment here, there seems to be some value in **not** explicitly determining this value, as there is an effort to set those pooled connections _very close_ to the connected threshold value without actually making them initially connected. [see this](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1129)

@vitaly-krugl Yes, regression tests are run after every push to nupic/master. See https://travis-ci.org/numenta/nupic.regression/builds. However, they are not run against pull requests (regression tests are by nature long-running tests). 

Many companies run nightly regression tests, which highlight failures once a day. Ours at least will run after each push to master, and when they fail an email is sent to nupic-hackers.

> The upwind finite difference scheme is incorrect for negative phi. In each
> dimension we want to choose the frozen neighbour with the smaller
> *magnitude* of distance from the zero contour, not the neighbour with the
> smallest value (for which neighbours further inside the structure, with
> more negative phi, will win).
>
> Owing to the nature of the algorithm, in which neighbours are updated
> outwards from the zero contour in a recursive fashion, this error only
> seems to cause issues for nodes lying close to the minimum of the level
> set, i.e. for regions in the middle of holes. The image below shows the
> position of nodes that are updated incorrectly (labeled as different)
> during level set optimisation of the classic 2:1 centrally loaded
> cantilever.

> results to fast marching methods that update regions with positive and
> negative phi separately. In a two-pass scheme, it is possible to use the
> updated level set variables from the first pass in order to obtain a more
> accurate estimate for the position of frozen nodes adjacent to the zero
> contour in the second pass, i.e. in the linear interpolatation of distances

Why are the user names not exported? 

Lot's of objects are connected to a certain user. If they are not known to tower, the import will not work at all.

> I think it would make more sense to have it follow how we do other resource creation/editing.

We do that with how the nodes work, but the problem with that system is that nodes don't have any named accessor that you can use. You have to save and store the pk. Nodes are unique in this way because of the Tower implementation.

Extrapolating a bit, but it sounds like what you want is an option `--deleting-existing=true`, which if set, will delete all the existing nodes in the WFJT before creating the data in the provided schema. It sounds like you might also want that to be on by default.

Hmmm.  Still a problem; in that chain of logic, we still potentially have 18 replicas trying to connect to one highest replica in the same couple seconds.  If the replicas are heavily loaded and near their connection limits, this could cause the highest to not respond.

This is a reason to go through the API, I think; we can have the API differentiate between "connection limit" and "down".  If it returns "connection limit", then the other replicas retry in a loop.

The alternative is to have the API have some kind of data caching, but that seems like more of a pain.

By common convention (rather than by enforcement), an assignment to a variable in caps signifies that it is to be used a constant value.  Dipping in to a package from another program/module/package and changing the value of such a package specific var is a practice that has risks and is to be generally avoided.  With that said, the Rally.get method offers a 'start' keyword that should be used whenever you want to qualify the 'get' to those items satisfying a query whose index in the qualifying sequence starts at an index other than 1.  When provided, the value associated with the 'start' keyword is used in constructing the overall query string for the request.  The Agile Central (Rally) WS REST API does not use base-offset indexing (where the index of the first item is 0), rather it uses positive natural numbers where the first item is index 1.  Information on all of the keyword arguments available for the Rally.get method is available in the package documentation.

This does the trick: 
```
import logging
import sys


def get_logger(name, level=logging.ERROR,
               format='%(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s'):
    """
    Gets a logger with the given name configured for lambda

    :param name: Name of the logger to retrieve
    :param level: Logging level to configure the logger for, either as a
    `logging` constant (e.g. `logger.DEBUG`) or a case-insensitive string
    corresponding to one of the logging constants, (e.g. `debug`).
    :param format: Log formatting string
    :return: Logger
    """
    handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
    # Timestamp is handled by lambda itself so the
    # default FORMAT_STRING doesn't need to include it.
    formatter = logging.Formatter(format)
    handler.setFormatter(formatter)

    log = logging.getLogger(name)
    log.propagate = False
    log.addHandler(handler)

    if type(level) == str:
        level = getattr(logging, level.upper())

    log.setLevel(level)

    return log
```

Now, when applying the following feature file:
```
feature test {
  sub [A-F] by [a-f];
} test;
```

makeotf produces a GSUB table with the following mapping, just as one would expect:
```
        <SingleSubst index="0">
          <Substitution in="A" out="a"/>
          <Substitution in="B" out="b"/>
          <Substitution in="C" out="c"/>
          <Substitution in="D" out="d"/>
          <Substitution in="E" out="e"/>
          <Substitution in="F" out="f"/>
        </SingleSubst>
```

Note that in the output, there is an E → e mapping even though the glyph ID of E is higher than that of F in the input test font. So it actually appears that makeotf does _not_ look at glyph IDs when interpreting the range expressions. In other words, makeotf’s implementation seems to be faithfully implementing the spec.

                                  total_bytes, auto_transfer=False,
