If you don't mind using [Apache Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) this is a [pom.xml that will create a launch script](https://gist.github.com/pfifer/d1e884cf83ff9b22d1e479c6a5cc4b72) that will setup the class path for you.  

To create the launcher script:

1. Create an empty directory
2. Create a new file in the directory called `pom.xml`
3. Install [Apache Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) if you don't already have it
4. In the directory created in 1, run the command `mvn package`
5. If everything works you should have a script in `target/appassembler/bin/multilang-daemon`, and all the jars are in `target/appassembler/repo`.  
6. Copy the directory `target/appassembler` to wherever you need it.
7. To start your application run the mutlilang-daemon script with the location of your property file.

Just in case, my own template that works great for my needs (2 stages, staging + prod):

`Resources:

  CheckStatusFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      FunctionName: nway-CheckStatus
      # <assembly file>::<namespace.classname>::<method name>
      Handler: nwayapi::nWAY.API::CheckStatus   
      Role: !Sub "arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/nway-lambdas"
  

  CheckStatusFunctionAPIMethod:
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::Method
    Properties:
      RestApiId: !Ref nWAYApi
      ResourceId: !Ref ResourceChecks
      HttpMethod: GET      
      AuthorizationType: NONE
      Integration:
        Type: AWS_PROXY
        IntegrationHttpMethod: GET
        Uri: !Sub "arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${CheckStatusFunction.Arn}:${!stageVariables.lambdaAlias}/invocations"


  ################################
  # API Gateway definition                                                                                 
  ################################

  nWAYApi: 
    Type: "AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi"
    Properties:
      Description: "nWAY API"
      Name: "nway-api"

  ################################
  # API resources                                    
  #   /checks                                        
  ################################

  ResourceChecks: 
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::Resource
    Properties: 
      RestApiId: !Ref nWAYApi
      ParentId: 
        Fn::GetAtt: 
          - nWAYApi
          - RootResourceId
      PathPart: "checks"

  ################################
  # deployment 'environments'                                  
  #   staging                                                   
  #   prod                                                        
  ################################

  # staging deployment
  Staging:
    Type: 'AWS::ApiGateway::Stage'
    DependsOn: nWAYApi
    Properties:
      StageName: staging
      Description: API Staging
      RestApiId: !Ref nWAYApi
      DeploymentId: !Ref StagingDeployment
      Variables:
        lambdaAlias: staging
  
  StagingDeployment: 
    DependsOn: 
      - CheckStatusFunctionAPIMethod
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment
    Properties: 
      RestApiId: !Ref nWAYApi
  
  # prod deployment
  Prod:
    Type: 'AWS::ApiGateway::Stage'
    DependsOn: nWAYApi
    Properties:
      StageName: prod
      Description: API Production
      RestApiId: !Ref nWAYApi
      DeploymentId: !Ref StagingDeployment
      Variables:
        lambdaAlias: prod

  ProdDeployment: 
    DependsOn: 
      - CheckStatusFunctionAPIMethod
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment
    Properties: 
      RestApiId: !Ref nWAYApi
`

Very interesting! I had only implemented `readable_by_user`, because that was the only one that was required in order to fulfill basic API endpoint filtering (for doing a "GET" on a list-view endpoint). Other, more specific filtering, was left to manual `FilterSet` classes at the API level (e.g. see `ContentNodeFilter`). I think there are a few reasons to keep this separation:
1. This filter won't actually end up being used for permission-checking, per-se (since at the time a record is being written, it will be calling one of the `user_can_*_object` methods). So, it would be better to not add this to BasePermissions -- as that also then sets the expectation that *all* permission classes need to define this.
2. As you say, it's not clear how this would integrate with Django Rest Framework (via KolibriAuthPermissionsFilter). Filtering in DRF is designed to filter the results of doing a "GET" on a list-view, and the permissions filter is intended to return all objects that the user is permitted to read.
2. I'm not sure your specific current use case (something related to only showing a coach a list of classes they also have admin privileges for?), but semantically, you probably care more about their role with respect to the class than their ability to edit the class object itself. The current approach seems focused on the latter, whereas a custom `FilterSet` object that allows filtering of the `classroom` endpoint as needed is probably a better way to accomplish the goal.

Happy to mock up an example, if you could describe in a couple of sentences the context it's needed for. Thanks!

https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/blob/6b8ac9dd6b38128d7fa0db96f0b4a51bbde2a09a/ipwb/replay.py#L512-L516

Let's try for the following simple and intuitive behavior:

* `ipwb replay` should print replay help to highlight that a CDXJ file argument is mandatory
* `ipwb index some.warc | ipwb replay` should utilize the resulting index received from the pipe for replay
* `ipwb replay sample.cdxj` should use `sample.cdxj` for replay

Let's get rid of the magical default index file on replay that has to go through a bootstrapping process of populating IPFS without explicitly calling indexing. Quick getting started behavior can be documented instead of hardcoding in the main code. We can suggest people to run `ipwb index ipwb/samples/warcs/salam-home.warc | ipwb replay` for a quick test. Also, the default command in our Dockerfile can have a similar default behavior.

Use `os.isatty()` instead of `sys.stdin.isatty()` to check if a pipe is present for input.

This is not something the ProfitBricks SDK will support. The SDK follows the behavior of the ProfitBricks REST API which is to accept `true` if you want DHCP enabled or `false` if you want DHCP disabled. The default behavior of both the SDK and REST API is to enable DHCP unless it is set to `false`. The `dhcp` property value should not be used to evaluate whether an object exists or not. That logic is better handled prior to defining the NIC.

If you are on linux or mac os, using pyenv, you can suppress the error with a small patch to the `databricks` shell script. You can leave a python 3 install as your global python version with something like `pyenv global 3.6.3`, depending on which python 3 version you've installed.  

1. `pyenv install` a python 2 version, e.g. `pyenv install 2.7.14`
1. `pyenv install` a python 3 version, e.g. `pyenv install 3.6.3`
1. `pyenv global` your python 2 version, e.g. `pyenv global 2.7.14`
1. `pip install databricks-cli`
1. Add an export to the `databricks` bash script at line 3 `export PYENV_VERSION=2.7.14`. You can locate the script for patching with `which databricks`
1. `pyenv global` back to your python 3 version, e.g. `pyenv global 3.6.3`

Something similar may work for Windows installations using `conda`.

This is not an issue of docker-py, instead it's an issue of either python package management or distribution package management. tl;dr is that you shouldn't mix those two ecosystems.

If a python package requires package X in version Y, and X-Y breaks other packages, that's not a fault of the former python package. It doesn't know what versions are available on the system, nor does it know what packages in which versions other packages require. That's the responsibility of package manager.

docker-py maintainers identified [range](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/setup.py#L12) of versions in which their package is working. Your version is not in that range.

Since the version of requests you have is pretty outdated. I would suggest installing docker-py in a new clean virtualenv or in a docker container (since newest requests break your host system).

Inspired by xray I used the terminology _coordinate_ for an index along a specific dimension and _index_ for the value passed to `get_frame`. The transformation index --> coordinates according to `iterate` gave me some headaches and is (as far as I could see) not implemented in xray.

I kicked out the `FramesDimension` object and I just use dictionaries `_sizes` and `_default_coords` and lists `_aggrate` and `_iterate` to keep track of all the dimension properties. For clarity, the default coordinate is the coordinate that is used if the dimension is both not in `aggregate` and `iterate`

I didn't want to use the `__init__` because if you subclass for instance `ImageSequence` and `FramesSequenceND` together, you want to keep both `__init__` routines. Therefore I thought it to be more elegant to solve the dimension initialization in `_dim_add` if `_sizes` does not exist.

@nklongvessa This is very exciting! I think that the other maintainers would all be very interested in these capabilities. We want to help people analyze experiments that were previously difficult, slow, or impossible. Right now trackpy works well to find and store the trajectories in large data sets (my own experiments have about 12k frames and 30k particles), but as you say, it cannot then perform the very important step of computing MSD.

My own inclination is to put each streaming function in the same file as its non-streaming counterpart. My least-preferred option is to put it in `framewise_data.py`; I think that module should be dedicated to data storage only.

I don't know if this is part of your plans, but once an algorithm has been made suitable for streaming it may be much easier to run on many cores in parallel. There has been a lot of interest in using `dask` to convert the entire trackpy "stack" to be as parallel as possible, beginning with reading images from the disk (see https://github.com/soft-matter/pims/pull/254 ), and ending with trajectory analysis such as your functions.

I am really looking forward to this contribution! Let us know if you have more questions.

I'm not so sure I agree. Deploying Deis is in itself a rather large cost commitment so I'd rather re-use what the platform provides; I want the 3 nodes to have a 80% CPU utilisation before I start firing up more of them. This is my current requirement, but that could change as we start getting more customers.

With the database url - there are two issues with it - the first is that it would mean adding another level of failover - and therefore a proxy in between, like [HAProxy + etcd](http://jasonwilder.com/blog/2014/07/15/docker-service-discovery/) -- except, now there are even more moving parts, especially so if I have a client that's capable of failover itself.

Another issue with introducing a service proxy is that if my client expects Strict Serializability on Write, but only guarantees it per-session (read-your-writes if you're connecting to the same instance), having a load-balanced solution could possibly void the consistency semantics of the database with such a consistency option.

I've done work with AWS RDS and it's the most terrible form of storage I can think of - it has high latency, varying latency and isn't available directly on boot -- which is why I would prefer using the local storage of the nodes that deis is deployed on instead. I'm planning on setting it up in DigitalOcean. Consul's SRV records would be a great way of doing it, but again, we have etcd - and the service discovery issue is still open.

I was planning on using Registrator with Consul + Health Checks before I found Deis - that way it's all automatic and I can place a watch on the record, to re-create the connection (pool) whenever it changes. It would be great if there could be something similar in Deis.

I chose this to be triggered by `/deis/router/enforceHTTPS` since it really is an extension of that: hsts forces the browser to use https on the domain for the specified number of seconds unless a subsequent request overwrites the maxAge. There should probably be a way to opt out of the hsts header but still enforce 301 redirects to https, even if the default is to trigger this with the same key.

@smothiki It's important to distinguish the API/model from the implementation. Kubernetes supports cpus. We currently translate cpus into shares, but plan to enhance the implementation in the future, such as using cpu masks and/or throttling to provide greater isolation for latency-critical applications. Some discussion of future extensions can be found in:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/blob/master/docs/resources.md
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/kubernetes/issues/147
It's also worth looking at our prior work:
http://static.googleusercontent.com/media/research.google.com/en/us/pubs/archive/43438.pdf
https://github.com/google/lmctfy

If shares are exposed directly, it's pretty important to have some notion of how many total shares should be allocated on a single node. Otherwise, performance can degrade arbitrarily once a node is significantly oversubscribed.

Also, while we support expression of resource values using floating-point values, we represent the values using integers internally. Using floating point to represent cpu caused us significant grief in Borg.

Yes, there is a condition where logs incoming from the docker socket could not be sent to the logger. Unfortunately I don't see a way around this as calling `router.Remove("etcd")` immediately following `router.Add()` will clear the new route we just added. We also cannot edit an existing route's `Target` in the routing table as that value is immediately shipped off [to a goroutine](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/logspout/routes.go#L83) which spawns a TCP connection, so that cannot be modified on the fly, only killed off with `.Remove`.

I think the best course of action would be to fetch the etcd route from the routing table, compare against the route we want to add and see if their targets are identical. If they differ then we can remove the old one since that route is stale and add the new one. If they're identical then it's a no-op. Does that seem fair?

Oops, as it turns out, it isn't quite as simple as I had thought :-S. If you're running a 64 bit kernel (aarch64), and a 32 bit user space (similar to how SPARC systems are set up) - armhf - everything will work (with the above changes to conda/base/context.py). If, however, you are running both a 64 bit kernel _and_ 64 bit userspace, you need to add the armhf architecture, and libc6:armhf (which provides the ld-linux-armhf.so.3 linker binary). For debian-based systems, the below works:

```bash
sudo dpkg --add-architecture armhf && sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install libc6:armhf
ln -s /path/to/ld-linux-armhf.so.3 <parent dir of ld-linux-aarch64.so.3>/
```

You may also need to add a symlink from <parent dir of ld-linux-aarch64.so.3>/ld-linux.so.3 to ld-linux.armhf.so.3.

The separate metapackage includes some critically important flexibility.  If you don't have the metapackage, but you depend only on the dependency, then your pinning in flang on the clangdev package becomes unnecessarily tight, and you might also have to depend on crazy things like pinning to a specific build string with hash.  The purpose of the metapackage is the mutex, but also to express the dependency portion of the hash in a more meaningful way.

I did just think of a possible way to solve the race condition: we could leave the file handles open until we try the unlink, then close them the line before unlinking.  I'm -0 on it though, because I'm afraid we'd hit the open file limit pretty quickly on some packages (i.e., pretty much every node package every since they all include about one million copies of underscore, lodash, and mkdirp).

You could try to do something like:

   conda update conda --no-update-dependencies

I don't know how well that might work - you may need to list additional dependencies, such as ruamel_yaml, explicitly.

> I have Curator version 5.2.0 installed via YUM

This is the problem.  The YUM package does not include the system python libraries/modules.  It is a standalone version for running only.

You need to install via `pip` in order to have the libraries accessible for API use.

```
pip list | grep curator
```
You should see what version is installed that way.

You can upgrade via:
```
sudo pip install -U elasticsearch-curator
```
or 
```
pip install -U --user elasticsearch-curator
```
to limit the installation to a local user (and not overwrite the system python libraries).  Just remember that only the user that ran this will have that version of the Curator module installed.

**PLEASE NOTE:** You should not have both the `pip` version _and_ the YUM package simultaneously installed, so as to avoid confusion when calling `curator` at the command-line.

This represents a slight misunderstanding of what happens under the hood.  A snapshot completing with state `FAILED` can still represent successful execution of the Snapshot API—which is exactly what happened, based on the log lines you provided.  In other words, `continue_if_exception` only catches code _exceptions,_ and one did not happen here.

Snapshot failure is never a good thing.  Do you know what's going on with this?  Have you checked the Elasticsearch logs to see why it's failing?  That might be a more pressing concern than altering this feature in Curator, as you're not even getting a `PARTIAL` snapshot state—it's outright saying the snapshot has `FAILED`. (See more on snapshot status codes [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/6.2/modules-snapshots.html#_snapshot)).

With that said, it might be a good feature to add an option flag to force Curator to _raise_ an exception if a snapshot doesn't complete with state `SUCCESS`, for example.

So it seems that all 3 of these are caused by the same issue - proper handling of the KeyboardInterrupt.
And the varying presentation depends on where exactly the event loop is at the moment of interrupt.
And it's kinda tricky to catch, because the code can be interrupted *anywhere*.

For example - in this function here:
[connection_async.work_async( )](https://github.com/Azure/azure-uamqp-python/blob/master/uamqp/async_ops/connection_async.py#L108)

Notice that the lock.acquire is called outside of the try/except. If the function is interrupted during lock acquisition, then the lock will not be released.

After making several adjustments I have reduced the number of hanging scenarios - so some progress at least.

On the other hand, scenario no. 3 is rather challenging - as this is happening when the interrupt occurs during asyncio event management. Still not sure how to debug this yet....

Yes, it would be a good idea to have more subcategories so that the class isn't so large :)

However, given that this is a legacy API (we are now pushing users and putting our efforts towards the new ARM APIs), I have stopped worrying about these old design decisions and accepted to live with them.  Avoiding breaking changes for users that don't want to switch to ARM is a higher priority.

So far we've only split by Azure service.  Scheduler, ServiceBus, SqlDatabase, WebSite were added as their own classes because they are separate services from what was in ServiceManagement (storage+compute).

VM publishing support is part of compute which already has a class.  It sure is large, but it would feel more out of place to separate it out versus integrating it in the existing class.

Note that the method names will have to change as they get integrated into the existing class, because `replicate` needs to be qualified ie. `replicate_vm_image` might be a good name.

That's my thinking.  I'm trying to keep things as consistent as they can be.

```
storage_resource_group = None

    if sa_id_parts[-1] == storage_account:
        storage_resource_group = sa_id_parts[4]

Regarding the constructor param in the `TCPConnector` remember that this is a replacement of the old cache system that didn't have a way to use all `addrs` during the happy path execution, therefore IMHO the `use_dns_cache` is, in fact, the related param to enable or disable this feature. Or were you thinking in another case?

@kxepal The idea behind this it's put some continuation in this other work [1], having an expiration of the  DNS table with an enough low value and having the chance to use all `addr` resolved by a host you will be in the best position to use services that have in front LBs that use the DNS as a way scale up or scale down. Take as an example the case of the ELBs orchestrated by AWS that you use in your daily work, they behave as it has been explained.

There are examples that show you how the software [2] spent some time to react to the change in the expiration latency, IMHO the app layer has to be ready to live in an environment where the resolutions can change quickly.

[1] https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/1819
[2] http://discourse.haproxy.org/t/trouble-with-dns-resolvers-sticking-to-single-ip-address/146/4

> From that perspective `convert` could become a private method `_convert`.

If we go that route, it could just be removed altogether in favor of `transforms.convert`.

But I'm hesitant to do either because it would be yet another compatibility break with no clear utility. It's presently impossible to mask the `convert` attribute anyway, so enforcing its reserved status won't break anyone's application, while removing the method will. Both `__init__` and `import_data` call `convert`, so it's all but guaranteed that there are custom implementations doing the same.

> Regarding `keys`, shouldn't having `__iter__` be enough for the dict constructor to work?

`dict()` first looks if the object has a `keys` attribute. If it exists, it *must* be a callable that returns an iterable of keys, otherwise the constructor blows up.

If `keys` does not exist, `dict()` will iterate over the object itself, but in that case it expects key–value pairs, not keys.

So, the only way to guarantee that `dict(someinstance)` works is to implement `keys()` and protect the attribute — or switch to iterating over key–value pairs, which would break the general mapping contract.

@duvenaud pointed this out because @alexbw and I had another idea for checkpointing like this:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd.core import primitive, make_vjp
import inspect

def checkpoint(fun):
    wrapped = primitive(fun)
    arglist = inspect.getargspec(fun).args
    for argnum, _ in enumerate(arglist):
        wrapped.defvjp(
            lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, *args: make_vjp(fun, argnum)(*args)[0](g),
            argnum=argnum)
    return wrapped

@checkpoint
def bar(x, y):
    return 2*x + y + 5

def foo(x):
    return bar(x, x/3.) + bar(x, x**2)

print foo(3.)
print grad(foo)(3.)
```

We haven't fully thought through this yet, so there could be something broken with the above code, but the basic idea is to define a decorator to do checkpointing at the function level. The strategy is to wrap the function as a primitive, so that autograd doesn't save nodes for the computations inside it, and then define the primitive's vjp by calling make_vjp on the (unwrapped) function. As a result, on the 'outer' backward pass the function's forward pass is re-run and re-traced.

I think (but I could be wrong) that this is more flexible than `checkpointed_grad` because it doesn't have anything in particular to do with chain compositions. That is, your code is effectively building a chain graph and then walking it, while this code lets autograd do all the graph building and walking. However, I might not fully understand your code yet, so correct me if I'm wrong.

A tradeoff could be that there is more autograd-internals-related overhead associated with this decorator approach, since autograd DAGs are, while more general than chains, more expensive to build and traverse than native lists. Maybe we want both of these tools.

Thoughts?

My current understanding is that to workaround the CMake configure error reported for [conda-forge tbb recipe](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/conda-forge/staged-recipes/build/1.0.2118/job/t2axdaotmty35j6k#L216) ,  you are applying the same hack that we now generalized in `ci/appveyor/driver.py`. 

and finally ... 

> skbuild (and CMake, ideally)  should look for [MS C++ Compiler for Python 2.7] first, and fall back to looking for VS. That's what distutils does.

Makes sense.

Since the `MS C++ Compiler for Python 2.7` provides:
* `vcvars.bat`, `vcvars86.bat` and `vcvars64.bat` 
* along side the the `nmake.exe` executable

=> CMake should already support it ! This is confirmed with the experiment results report below.

Conclusion: There is no need to change CMake, it supports `MS C++ Compiler for Python 2.7` out of the box.

What does this mean for `scikit-build` ? 

* When compiling on Windows with Python 2.7:
  1. Look for `C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\Visual C++ for Python\9.0\vcvars.bat`
  2. Depending on the bitness of python interpreter, set the env calling either `vcvars.bat x86` or `vcvars.bat x64`  (or `C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\Visual C++ for Python\9.0\VC\bin\vcvars(64|86).bat` if more convenient)
  3. Invoke CMake to configure  (Specify `-DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release`)
  4. Then invoke CMake --build /path/to/proj-build

If `Visual C++ for Python` is not available, suggest to download and install it.

Case where applying the workaround would potentially be needed:

So, reading through all these notes, I'm wondering what the way forward is.

Visual Studio is the toolchain suggested for Python, but @jcfr's suggestion for scikit-build is to deviate from the recommended generators and use NMake?  Should we do this across the board for Windows builds?

If we have Visual Studio as well as the Compiler for Python, should we not be able to use both?  My read of all this so far is that this is the scenario that currently requires registry mods, but @patricksnape's suggestion is that such mods would not be necessary if the VS2008 generator in CMake can be updated to look for msbuild.exe, just as the VS2010 generator does?

If this understanding is right, then it seems to me that this issue should be upstreamed to the CMake team.  On our end, we have multiple options for working around the issue (including doing nothing, btw, since we are already using the registry fix when testing), but there is still evidence of an improvement that can be made to CMake, proper.

So, perhaps we should proceed on two fronts:
  1. Prepare and submit an issue to CMake to detect and use the C++ Compiler for Python in the VS2008 Generator.
  1. Explore a better work around in scikit-build while we wait for the improvement to land, the better work around most likely being a temporary switch to the NMake Generator.

Then, after a fix lands in CMake, we can undo the switch and go back to using VS generators across the board?

> This is actually pretty problematic. They're called by the API server, and then many of the calls in there call back to the API server, causing function call graphs that invoke the call in actions.py twice (cli_register for example), and obfuscate or mangle option defaults. In general, the args object doesn't handle default well, and makes it so that if a function call wants to specify say the second optional argument, it has to also specify the first. This isn't good long term. The API server should be invoking methods that look more like methods, and the CLI should interact through the API server.

I definitely agree that this aspect of the codebase needs to be refactored.  We originally did it this way because we wanted read-only methods (like `whois` and `lookup`) to work without requiring the user to unlock their wallet.  However, this was during a time when the CLI was the *only* interface to the system, and the background daemon was only responsible for registering names.

Since this is no longer the case, and since users will be expected to be running an API daemon all the time anyway, the current call graph no longer makes sense.  Nevertheless, changing the structure of `actions.py` is going to be a somewhat big undertaking.  We should attack it once our interfaces are stable to minimize conflicts (i.e. after multi-reader Gaia and the subdomain registrar are merged in, and once we're happy with the 1.0 API for core.blockstack.org).  Just my $0.02.

I think it might be safer to use random state instances, instead of setting the global seed. If someone uses the mesa framework and sets global random seeds in their own implementation, they will unknowingly influence the random generators in the mesa implementation, which might lead to unwanted behaviour. In addition, setting the global seed might not be thread-safe, and it could very well be that somebody works with multiple threads with mesa.

I can make a PR with the proposed changes. (Same thing goes for the space module, where also the global random generator was used.)

> It looks like the confusion is in the naming of the `iterations` argument.

My confusion isn't much in the name of the `iterations` argument but in the meaning of it, because of the `max_steps` argument. If the `iterations` argument is the number of runs of a model, then a model will run at most `iterations` or `n_runs` (if you prefer) if `self.running = True` without  the `max_steps` argument. But with the `max_steps` argument, the model will run at most `iterations * max_steps` if `self.running = True`.

> ...since some models may not have end conditions, or may not always reach them.

I think the number of iterations or runs is the "ultimate" end condition.

Ok here is the thing:

It is **not** possible to perform a `convertLead` if there exist at least one **custom, required** field in one of the target objects (*i.e.* `Contact`, `Account` or `Opportunity`). It was maybe possible to do that in previous versions of SF but not anymore. Some evidences:

![custom_required_fail](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6845103/6145584/bbdf4bfc-b1e3-11e4-8091-b5726ef3596f.png) 
(the message you get when you try to set a custom field as required in `Contact`)

and the error message from `beatbox`:

> System.DmlException: Insert failed. First exception on row 0; first error: REQUIRED_FIELD_MISSING, Required fields are missing: [My_Field__c]: [My_Field__c] (System Code)UNKNOWN_EXCEPTION

Two solutions:

1. Map custom Lead fields to the custom required fields on the target objects.
    * Then you need to create a custom required field in Lead for every custom required fields in the target objects. Should not be a problem in my opinion.
2. Define a default value for this custom required field in case no value is provided. But then we can legitimately wonder why this field is "required".

In both scenarios I believe `django-salesforce` could at least warn the user regarding this issue, both in the comments and in the logs. Any thought?

I find out this is caused by the share of start and end position instance when the start and end is the same. In hgvs parser, when the start and end is same, they will use the same instance. Thus, when changing the end position of such variants in the normalizer, the start position will also be changed. This leads to this bug.

Example code:
```python
import hgvs
import hgvs.parser
import hgvs.normalizer
import hgvs.dataproviders.uta

hdp = hgvs.dataproviders.uta.connect()

hp = hgvs.parser.Parser()

hn = hgvs.normalizer.Normalizer(hdp)

var_g = 'NC_000001.10:g.1647893delinsCTTTCTT'

var = hp.parse_hgvs_variant(var_g)

var.posedit.pos.start is var.posedit.pos.end
```
`>>>True`
```python
var_g = 'NC_000001.10:g.1647893_1647894delinsCTTTCTT'

var = hp.parse_hgvs_variant(var_g)

var.posedit.pos.start is var.posedit.pos.end
```
`>>>False`


I think the start and end position should always be two different instances no matter the start and end positions are equal or not.

I think you have the names for collections and databases upside down: In mongo you create a database and each database can have several collections (think tables).

In the old version, each prefix had their own filestorage. Therefore there were 3 collections for each prefix. The motivation for the new version/interface was to share the filestorage within a database across "prefixes". 

So in the new version you would still have 3 collections if you run with a single prefix: `fs.files`, `fs.chunks` and `foo` (which is not used as a prefix anymore so I renamed it to collection). But once you add another prefix (say `bar`) there will only be one additional collection `bar` that shares the files with `foo`. That way you can organize your experiments in different collections without duplicating the stored sources/resources.

> Does anyone know exactly what that does?

It deletes your current `~/.theano/compiledir-...` which caches compiled modules generated by Theano when compiling a function or when activating GPU support. When you update your CUDA version or graphics driver, it may happen that the GPU support module cannot be imported any longer as it links to earlier versions of system libraries, thus `import theano` fails, thus `from . import nonlinearities` fails, thus `import lasagne` fails. Forcing a recompile by deleting the theano cache is the easiest solution then.

Hi Gennadiy,

Relying on `__del__` to close things may seem like a no-brainer, but it is always going to be a worse solution than explicitly closing the filesystems. The reason I gave above will all apply to your use-case. You're most likely going to get a reference cycle with filesystem / file, and sockets  etc. will not be closed until whenever the gc kicks in.

Your example is interesting, but keeping track of the filesystems and closing them should not prohibitive. Not free, but much simpler than the streaming zip you implemented.

> I just say, that FileSystems2 is supposed to provide abstraction layer to remote file systems and behave just like local file system (open file object, close it and do not worry about closing the file system itself). 

I'm not sure that is applicable. It's true that devs don't typically manage the life cycle of the local file system, but it still needs to be managed. It's just that you rely on the OS to close the filesystem for you when you shut down. Since the OS isn't aware of PyFilesystem you have to do that manually.

Will

Unfortunately the CFG's graph is not supposed (or at least wasn't intended) to be deep-copied at all. Instead of making a deep copy of everything inside the CFG's graph (`CFGNodes` here), is it enough for you to create a shallow copy when creating the reversed graph? You can do it by creating a shallow copy of the networkx DiGraph instance first, and then reverse the new graph in-place. Try the following code snippet:

```
g = networkx.DiGraph(cfg.graph) # Make a shallow copy of the graph
g.reverse(copy=False) # In-place graph reversing
```

Ref: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29854387/networkx-copy-clarification

Please let me know if you really want to make a deep copy of the CFG graph itself - which I don't understand why for now, and there might be other implications.

You can just set the `record.string` to a unicode string (i.e. `unicode` type in Python 2, or `str` type in Python 3), and let fonttools encode it automatically for you with the correct platform encoding for that name record.

I also recommend to use `from __future__ import unicode_literals` whenever you can, so your string literals are implicitly treated as unicode strings (without needing `u""` in front of them) like in Python 3.

The `fontTools.misc.py23` module has some convenience utilities and type aliases to correctly work with unicode/str/bytes in a py2/py3 environment.

Remember that the list of command line arguments that you get from the console (`sys.argv`) are `bytes` strings on Python 2, and these are encoded with the console's encoding (`UTF-8` on most Unix based systems). On Python 3 on the other hand, they are already decoded to unicode strings (`str`). In the case of python 2, you'll need to know what the console's encoding is (`sys.stdout.encoding` gives you a hint, but the latter may be `None` if standard streams have been redirected to a pipe; you can google for "python 2 sys.argv encoding" or if you feel lazy use "utf-8" all the time!), and then use the console's encoding to decode the arguments to get the unicode strings.

There's a `tounicode` function in `fontTools.misc.py23` that decodes a bytes string to unicode with a given encoding or returns the string as is if it is already a unicode string; useful in these kinds of situations where the input may be either bytes or unicodes and you want unicodes. (also check out `tobytes` and `tostr` functions in same module).

Hi @ljc19800331 ,

> 1: Is there a better way for to get textured 3D pointcloud in pyrealsense?

tl;dr: You should use `points.get_texture_coordinates()`:
```py
pc = rs.pointcloud()
pc.map_to(color)
points = pc.calculate(depth)
vtx = np.asanyarray(points.get_vertices())
tex = np.asanyarray(points.get_texture_coordinates())   # <----------- Like this
```

In details:
Please note that `pc.map_to(color)` does not change the color image, it takes the color and maps it to the point cloud as its texture, using the calibration data of the streams that created these images (which you are lacking in your code, thus have misalignment). 

After calling `pc.map_to(color)` you should call `points.get_texture_coordinates()` which returns a buffer protocol object that can be put into a `numpy` array. This array should be in the same size as `points` and its `i`th element contains the texture coordinates (`[u,v]`) of the `i`th element in the vertices array. The texture coordinates are the coordinate (valued in the range [0-1]) of the pixel in the color image you used in `map_to`.

> 2. Is there a direct way I can get the extrinsic parameters for the camera?

Yes :)
I assume you want to de/project by yourself, so you will also need the intrinsics parameters and the depth scale. The SDK maps RealSense device in the following hierarchy:
```
Device
 |___ Sensor 1
         |___ Stream 1  # Stream Profile 1, one of video/motion/pose stream
         |___ ...
         |___ Stream N # Stream Profile N, one of video/motion/pose stream
 |___ ...
 |___ Sensor M
```
Each stream profile is either a video, motion, or pose stream (at the moment). Video streams profiles provide intrinsics of cameras and motion stream profiles provide motion device intrinsics.
Inside the library we keep the extrinsic data between the streams, and you can ask each stream to get its transformation (extrinsics) to another stream. 

Here's how to get this data in python and also some additional data, and finally mapping a depth pixel to a color one:

```py
import pyrealsense2 as rs
pipeline = rs.pipeline()
pipe_profile = pipeline.start()
frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
color_frame = frames.get_color_frame()
color_frame.get_frame_metadata(rs.frame_metadata.actual_exposure)

depth_intrin = depth_frame.profile.as_video_stream_profile().intrinsics
color_intrin = color_frame.profile.as_video_stream_profile().intrinsics
depth_to_color_extrin = depth_frame.profile.get_extrinsics_to(color_frame.profile)
depth_sensor = pipe_profile.get_device().first_depth_sensor()
depth_scale = depth_sensor.get_depth_scale()
depth_pixel = [240, 320]
depth_point = rs.rs2_deproject_pixel_to_point(depth_intrin, depth_pixel, depth_scale)
color_point = rs.rs2_transform_point_to_point(depth_to_color_extrin, depth_point)
color_pixel = rs.rs2_project_point_to_pixel(color_intrin, color_point)
pipeline.stop()
```

Rectification is done by the ASIC and introduces a homography transformation from Y16 to Y8 view. 
It does not affect the distortion model of the frame, just reprojecting them into a shared virtual plane.

It helped. I could finally implement two cameras. Let me share my code below. 

Again, I started from the _opencv_viewer_example.py_. The added functions are that there are two cameras working at the same time, and that you can save the images by pressing 's'.

```
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
import logging


# Configure depth and color streams...
# ...from Camera 1
pipeline_1 = rs.pipeline()
config_1 = rs.config()
config_1.enable_device('013102060174')
config_1.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 640, 480, rs.format.z16, 30)
config_1.enable_stream(rs.stream.color, 640, 480, rs.format.bgr8, 30)
# ...from Camera 2
pipeline_2 = rs.pipeline()
config_2 = rs.config()
config_2.enable_device('046112051680')
config_2.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 640, 480, rs.format.z16, 30)
config_2.enable_stream(rs.stream.color, 640, 480, rs.format.bgr8, 30)


# Start streaming from both cameras
pipeline_1.start(config_1)
pipeline_2.start(config_2)

try:
    while True:

        # Camera 1
        # Wait for a coherent pair of frames: depth and color
        frames_1 = pipeline_1.wait_for_frames()
        depth_frame_1 = frames_1.get_depth_frame()
        color_frame_1 = frames_1.get_color_frame()
        if not depth_frame_1 or not color_frame_1:
            continue
        # Convert images to numpy arrays
        depth_image_1 = np.asanyarray(depth_frame_1.get_data())
        color_image_1 = np.asanyarray(color_frame_1.get_data())
        # Apply colormap on depth image (image must be converted to 8-bit per pixel first)
        depth_colormap_1 = cv2.applyColorMap(cv2.convertScaleAbs(depth_image_1, alpha=0.5), cv2.COLORMAP_JET)

        # Camera 2
        # Wait for a coherent pair of frames: depth and color
        frames_2 = pipeline_2.wait_for_frames()
        depth_frame_2 = frames_2.get_depth_frame()
        color_frame_2 = frames_2.get_color_frame()
        if not depth_frame_2 or not color_frame_2:
            continue
        # Convert images to numpy arrays
        depth_image_2 = np.asanyarray(depth_frame_2.get_data())
        color_image_2 = np.asanyarray(color_frame_2.get_data())
        # Apply colormap on depth image (image must be converted to 8-bit per pixel first)
        depth_colormap_2 = cv2.applyColorMap(cv2.convertScaleAbs(depth_image_2, alpha=0.5), cv2.COLORMAP_JET)

        # Stack all images horizontally
        images = np.hstack((color_image_1, depth_colormap_1,color_image_2, depth_colormap_2))

        # Show images from both cameras
        cv2.namedWindow('RealSense', cv2.WINDOW_NORMAL)
        cv2.imshow('RealSense', images)
        cv2.waitKey(1)

        # Save images and depth maps from both cameras by pressing 's'
        ch = cv2.waitKey(25)
        if ch==115:
            cv2.imwrite("my_image_1.jpg",color_image_1)
            cv2.imwrite("my_depth_1.jpg",depth_colormap_1)
            cv2.imwrite("my_image_2.jpg",color_image_2)
            cv2.imwrite("my_depth_2.jpg",depth_colormap_2)
            print "Save"


finally:

    # Stop streaming
    pipeline_1.stop()
    pipeline_2.stop()
```

There are couple of factors that have significant effect on RealSense performance:
a. Cables length and quality
b. Power (ZR300 in particular is quite power-hungry)
c. USB controller

I don't think there is anything wrong with cpp-capture software: The difference between cpp-capture and cpp-config-ui is that the former is trying to synchronize the streams based on timestamp (using wait_for_frame API), while the later does not (it uses direct callbacks with no synchronization). 
My theory is that your device produce corrupted frame headers, causing loss of frame timestamps. 

Regarding kernel warnings, there seem to be some conflicting information. Assuming you apply the latest patch from the master branch, you can see that the reported pixel formats are included in the headers. 
However, even if the patch was not applied for some reason, most of ZR300 functionality should not be affected on Ubuntu 16. You should be able to get Depth, Color, IR, Fish-eye and motion out-of-the-box, without any patches at all.

Hey @samvit, using a `List` for listing items in a collection is also supported.

However if you plan to use pagination in this collection, using a relay `ConnectionField` should be the path to go, as it provides builtin structures for handling pagination (`pageInfo`, `cursors`, ...), cursor based pagination, and so on.

You can still use a `List` with pagination if you need to, but you would need to implement the pagination for it.


You can achieve it with something similar to this:

```python
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    all_users = List(User, page=graphene.Int())

    def resolve_all_users(self, args, context, info):
        page_size = 10
        offset = args.get('page', 0) * page_size

        # The following will only be available in types inheriting from SQLAlchemyObjectType
        user_query = User.get_query(context)

        return user_query.offset(offset).limit(page_size)
```

Hope this helps!

I did not see the "coap" level duplicate detection - thanks for pointing that out.

The android app sends the *SAME* message ID.

The AGENT does not recognize the duplicate, and creates a *NEW* message ID.

In fact, I think the AGENT probably should *NEVER* do duplicate detection.  Instead *only* the end point should perform duplicate detection. Rational:  Lets say the forwarded/proxied message (Agent->NCP->Leader) got lost because some intermediary did not receive the message, or did not have buffers for the message.  The Proxy *COULD* - perform a retry operation... but I don't think *both* the Android App & the Agent should perform retries.

@tleonhardt: First, from the philosophical point of view: If the interpreter is able to understand the abbreviated command "ops,l" why should I have to ask the interpreter what it understands anyway (namely: &ops[tab]   --> opsjuggle)? Instead, if on tabbing the interpreter would step backwards through the history list  and would offer me **complete input lines** which would match my token - this would be great, because pressing [tab] or arrow keys is easier than pressing [Ctrl-r], because I can tab or arrow without having to look at the keyboard.

Second, the workflow: The commands are "atoms" for data processing which must be adapted to the experimental data in interactive sessions. I rely on the fact that the interpreter tracks the history so I do not have to write down the variants in order to remember them. I simply can discriminate them by abbreviating differently. Then I can address them by querying the input-line buffer (below: Variant 1 starts with "ops," and variant 2 with "opsj,"). Each experimental run has several hundred frames. I have to find a good compromise for evaluation of a complete set. Let's read the first frame
saskia> read,y012_00001_0001,2
A "variant 1":
saskia> ops,l&del,x0.05,x0.34&front,2,,10,2&gra
A "variant 2":
saskia> opsj,l&del,x,0.05&front,,,10,2&comb&median,3&gra
do_graphics shows the data for inspection. Optimize the sequence of atoms and their parameters by inspecting several of the frames which belong to the set. Different abbreviations make it easy to address the different variants. Then transfer the optimum sequence (reading, manipulating operations, writing with new name) to a cmd2-script which may call other cmd2-scripts. This is done for batch processing of the complete set of frames.

## prep.lrg: Preprocessing for TPU y01
&read,$1
&del,x0.05,x0.34
&front,,,10,2
# call smequi.lrg
@smequi
&write,ex$1

Such scripts also document the evaluation steps. In the end of the first step of data evaluation a super-script doit.lrg calls prep.lrg with each frame file:

# script doit.lrg
@prep,y012_00001_0001
@prep,y012_00001_0002
@prep,y012_00001_0003
.
.
@prep,y012_00001_0399
@prep,y012_00001_0400

Remark: Every kind of required parameterization can be achieved by positional arguments to the commands. Flags are not necessary. "$1" is a placeholder for the first positional argument of the script call. On the interpreter level all arguments are treated as strings. It is great, that cmd2 can be made to immediately return to the user prompt when a command fails inside a script.

Ok - note ther typo in docs.

> I tried to implement it to allow nullptr or py::none() ...

You should be able to add these two constructors to `cpp_function` to make both approaches work:

```C++
    cpp_function(std::nullptr_t) { }
    cpp_function(none) { }
```

It means you have to pass a literal `nullptr`, not just a null pointer of some other type, but it appears to work (though I only tested under gcc; I suppose it's possible that some other compilers in the CI suite have trouble with it).

That said, we probably don't need both here; I think sticking with just the `nullptr_t` argument support (i.e. not adding the `none` constructor) is enough.

This all sounds pretty good to me.  Breaking up of test_python_types was definitely the next logical step after dispersing test_issues.

I'm more lukewarm on coalescing common types.  I worry a bit that this is going to run into conflicts if pushed too far because future tests are going to want to change something something which in turn breaks other tests.

As for the `m.` everywhere, I have to say I prefer the `from m import x, y, z` rather than having dozens of `m.x()`, `m.y()`, etc., particular when functions are often repeated (for example, in `test_single_char_arguments()`).  I think this should be on more of a per-test basis: sometimes importing is going to be nicer, sometimes just using `m.whatever()` will be nicer (in much the same way that a localized `using namespace detail;` can make some code cleaner).

As for moving into submodules, I think that's a great idea: I used to run into cases where `cmake -DPYBIND11_TEST_OVERRIDE=whatever` didn't work properly because a test relied on something set in one of the other tests.  I think most of those are gone now, but isolating things to separate modules will make sure of that.

Hi @xJustx!

Unfortunately, to get this solved, I'm going to need a bit more info from you.  The thing I need is a "core file" -- it's what the kernel spits out when a program segfaults.  It's possible that it could contain private information, so I'm going to also ask you to encrypt it for your protection before you send it along.

To get certbot to dump out a core file, open up a new terminal and run the command `ulimit -c 585938`.  This will cause certbot to dump out a core file.  Then, run the command that produces the problem: `certbot renew -vvvv`.

Once it segfaults, you should see a file called `core` in your current directory.  Compress it by running `gzip core`.  Then we need to encrypt it.  You should have my encryption key on your computer already; check if `ls -l /usr/share/keyrings/debian-keyring.gpg` returns a file.  If not, install it with `apt install debian-keyring`.

Once you have that, encrypt your core file with the command `gpg -e -r hlieberman@debian.org --keyring /usr/share/keyrings/debian-keyring.gpg core.gz`.  That will give you an encrypted core file with the name `core.gz.gpg` or similar.  If that file is less than 10Mb, go ahead and email it to me.  If it's larger, put it up at your favorite filehost and email me the link to it.  The log file for that particular run would also be awesome.

To run tests for a single file/module, find the tests for that module and just run `python path/to/file_test.py`. The convention for where these test files is located is described at the top of https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#testing.

I ended up with a behemoth that uses a queue to store stdout and a reader that reads the queue.

```
def output_reader(proc, outq):
    for line in iter(proc.stdout.readline, b''):
        if len(line) > 0:
            sys.stderr.write("%s %s" % (time.time(), line))
            outq.put(line)
    sys.stderr.write("stdout closed, process finished...?")


@pytest.mark.timeout(45)
def test_cli_keystore_path(blockchain_private_keys, blockchain_services, tmpdir):
    geth_dir = os.path.join(tmpdir, encode_hex(blockchain_private_keys[0])[:8])
    keystore_path = os.path.join(geth_dir, 'keystore')
    password_file_path = os.path.join(geth_dir, 'pw')
    print("Reading password file...")
    with open(password_file_path, 'r') as f:
        print(f.readlines())

    print(keystore_path)

    proc = subprocess.Popen(['raiden',
                             "--keystore-path", keystore_path,
                             "--password-file", password_file_path,
                             "--datadir", geth_dir,
                             "--nat=none",
                             "--no-sync-check",
                             "--eth-rpc-endpoint", "http://127.0.0.1:29870",
                             "--network-id", "627"],
                            stdin=subprocess.PIPE,
                            stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
                            stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
                            universal_newlines=True,
                            encoding='UTF-8')
    outq = queue.Queue()

    t = threading.Thread(target=output_reader, args=(proc, outq))
    t.start()

    test_completed = False
    cnt = 0

    while not test_completed:
        print("Processing queue... %s" % time.time())
        try:
            for line in iter(outq.get_nowait, None):
                print('{0} got from queue: {1}'.format(time.time(), line), end='')
                outq.task_done()
                if "Select one" in '{0}'.format(line):
                    print("found it")
            print("Queue processed")
        except Exception as e:
            print("Empty queue")
            print(str(e))
        finally:
            cnt += 1
            print(cnt, outq.qsize())
            if cnt == 150:
                test_completed = True
        time.sleep(0.25)
    print("Outside the loop...")
    t.join(1)
    proc.terminate()
    try:
        proc.wait(timeout=0.2)
        print('== subprocess exited with rc =', proc.returncode)
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        print('subprocess did not terminate in time')

    assert "a" == "b"
```

Yes you don't have to use channels but I would strongly discourage not to use them. It leads to unstructured and much harder to use. We will be gradually converting all drivers shipped with QCoDeS to use channels where it makes sense. As soon as you write code with the old pattern and want to switch channel you will end up writing ugly code such as `getattr(inst, 'ch{}_parameter'.format(channel_number).get()` and so on this is so much cleaner with channels. 

Using the Channels is not that much of an additional complication it's mainly a matter of moving all the channel specific parameters to the channel class and then add the channels to the instrument with code like the following

```python
        # channel-specific parameters
        channels = ChannelList(self, "ScopeChannels", TPS2012Channel, snapshotable=False)
        for ch_num in range(1, 3):
            ch_name = "ch{}".format(ch_num)
            channel = TPS2012Channel(self, ch_name, ch_num)
            channels.append(channel)
        channels.lock()
        self.add_submodule("channels", channels)
```

You do currently need to subclass ArrayParameter as soon as you want a parameter that returns an array, this is because you manually need to specify how to load the data and the x-axis.
 
But that is not to say that you cannot start just with a Instrument class and implement all the StandardParameter, such as setting horizontal axis and so on and then implement the Channels and Arrayparameters later on

I understand that it's not a good practice to use too much multithreading in a CPython program (especially when it's computation intensive). But this PR can still be useful in some aspects:
1) It fixes the bug that main thread, import thread and flush-profile-data thread are all using the local scheduler client without proper protection.
2) IO intensive Python code. One use case is that we want to run a gPRC server in ray. What the server does is simply accepting a request, forward it to an actor and wait. In this case, it makes sense to use multi-threading. Because otherwise we'll need to spawn many actors, and each of them can only handle a request at a time.
3) Java worker. Because java doesn't have the GIL, and the overhead of JVM is big.

> undocumented API v3

You're mixing things up here a bit... Both public and private Twitch APIs are being used.

There are two supported versions of the public `/kraken` namespace right now, namely v2 and v3. When no API version is specified in the request, the latest one is always assumed. Setting an explicit version header will ensure that nothing will break once a newer version gets released. See https://github.com/justintv/Twitch-API

The private namespace that's being used is `/api` and also `tmi.twitch.tv` for checking for hosted channels. See https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/ef3476952fa9e6fa66f50e0fa515cabe070f7360/src/streamlink/plugins/twitch.py#L193-L218

I think there are a couple of API calls that could be changed so that the public namespace is used instead of the private one. I might be wrong, though.

I managed to figure out a work around for this bug.

1. Open up your streamlinkrc or equivalent streamlink config file.
2. Look for this piece of text: 

> \# Use this if you want streamlink to only pass a URL to your player and
\# let it handle the transport of the stream itself.
\#player-passthrough=http,hls,rtmp

Remove the hashtag from `#player-passthrough=http,hls,rtmp` and save the config file. Volia, you can now seek.

I think we should clarify in the title and copy that we are talking about output widgets. How about something like this for the copy. I've made very slight wording changes based on what you'd written:

```markdown
### Interacting with output widgets from background threads

Output widgets capture output based on the thread in which they were invoked.
In other words, in the thread where the output is invoked, there is a context
manager which starts and then stops the output capturing. If you call `display`
in a thread other than the thread containing the context manager, you cannot
rely on those `display` calls being captured by the context manager.

Instead, we can define and display a box widget in the main thread, and add
output widgets to it from a secondary worker thread. This does not break the
context manager that handles communication between the kernel and the frontend,
and it will work reliably every time. It operates on a similar concept to the
previously discussed task of modifying a widget's `value` in the background.
```

The reason `SocketStream` has an `aclose` method is because it implements the generic stream interface, and the generic stream interface has an `aclose` method. For example, this makes it easy to write code that works over both raw sockets and over SSL/TLS-encrypted sockets, because they export the same interface. The generic stream `aclose` method has the semantics that it attempts a graceful close – whatever that means for the particular concrete implementation – and if that fails/is cancelled, then it performs a forceful close. It happens that for a simple socket, graceful and forceful close are the same, so that's what `SocketStream.aclose` does. For `SSLStream`, they are different. (And in fact these `aclose` semantics are from `trio.abc.AsyncResource`, which is also implemented by other types beyond streams, like listeners and async files.)

Ah, I think I see the problem... The docs are not as clear as they could be about an important issue :-)

The intended way to use nurseries (unless you're actually implementing a custom supervisor) is that you spawn some tasks and then sit parked in the nursery `__aexit__`. The task that holds the nursery shouldn't be doing other work itself; it should all be delegated to children. ("Parenting is a full time job.")

I think this needs to be emphasized more in the docs, and in particular the tutorial... I'm seeing now that it's really not obvious (esp. since the call to `__aexit__` is literally invisible!). But even though from the user point of view it looks like the parent task isn't doing anything, in fact it's performing the critical work of watching for child tasks to exit, reaping them, and responding to any unexpected exceptions that bubble up from the children. (I think this is also why you were having trouble with `KeyboardExit`not working.)

Basically the nursery `__aexit__` **is** your autoreap function and more :-). (In particular, the snippet you show silently discards any unexpected exceptions; if you look at the code that implements nurseries then like 90% of the complications are from dealing with exceptions. Exceptions are super annoying.)

> Doesn't this mean each call to `__getattr__` also returns a different instance of that method? Calling the method factory for each getattr seems a bit wasteful.

I'm not really worried about the overhead, given that each method call is also creating a thread :-). I guess it's a bit weird maybe if `foo.meth is foo.meth` returns `False`. If it matters then the simple solution is to have `__getattr__` save the computed attribute the first time it's called, so the next time it gets pulled from the instance dict instead of calling `__getattr__`. (Maybe that's phrased confusingly. I'm talking about having `__getattr__` do `value = ...; setattr(self, attr, value); return value`.) An LRU cache would be overkill here I think.

> I also thought it was a bit too tedious to sort out the methods manually, so after reading pathlib#L606-L607, I decided to automatically compute `_forward` and `_wrap` based on whether this method is defined in `PurePath` or `Path`.

Hmm. This makes me twitch a bit because it's [extremely close to the sororicide pattern](https://glyph.twistedmatrix.com/2017/05/the-sororicide-antipattern.html) but I think maybe in this specific case it's OK?

From a glance at your work-in-progress though it seems like you might be overcomplicating it a bit though – we only need to wrap `pathlib.Path`, nothing else. (I don't know why python documents `WindowsPath` and `PosixPath` as always being available; in any given install only one of them is usable, and it's the one aliased to `Path`.) And we can compute the `_forwards` and `_wraps` sets like:

```python
_forwards = [attr for attr in dir(pathlib.PurePath) if not attr.startswith("_")]
_wraps = [attr for attr in dir(pathlib.Path) if not attrs.startswith("_") and attr not in _forwards]
```

and then the actual forwarding code can be identical to the code for files, except that we need the `if isinstance(result, pathlib.Path): result = Path(result)` check.

@Techcable  - Ownership of the domain is not a mandatory check by maven. The group ID is encouraged to be a unique piece of string and a domain is a good and standard way to ensure that. 

The issue I see with github being part of the groupid as it "dilutes" the domain concept as github is more of a hosting site as opposed to being the "owner". Hence I think the proposal for org.ninia.jep makes more sense.

There are many references in the maven central which do not follow this convention ( ex: log4j ). Maven naming conventions guide recommends to use the domain name as a good way to ensure uniqueness. Some interesting links in this this context here : 
- https://maven.apache.org/guides/mini/guide-naming-conventions.html 
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19924980/groupid-of-maven-projects-hosted-on-github

Hi @arnaudgelas,

You hit a python-gitlab problem: `GroupProject` objects should probably not inherit from `Project` because they don't reflect the same API. And thus they should not expose a `save()` method.

That being said, a simple solution is to create lazy `Project` objects in your loop, and use their `save()` method:

```
g = gl.groups.get(group_id)
projects = g.projects.list(all=True)
for group_p in projects:
    p = gl.projects.get(group_p.id, lazy=True)
    p.default_branch = 'develop'
    p.save()
```

Let me know if this works for you.

```shell
# for a machine **without** an NVIDIA GPU

# for a machine **with** an NVIDIA GPU
pip install -e .[all_gpu_dev] --upgrade
```
